Representative_Docs: ['• On Monday, the Lucas County Commissioners voted to grant $75,000 to the Lucas County Fair after previously voting down a request for $100,000 to the fair in April. Commissioner Lopez criticized the other commissioners for adding the item to the agenda less than an hour before the meeting and not discussing it with her since she raised concerns about the "no" vote in April. She also requested a public meeting be held about the status of the proposed new Lucas County Jail. • On Tuesday, 9.4% of voters turned out in the primary election. In the race for Mayor, incumbent Wade Kapszukiewicz (53%) and Roberto Torres (24%) outranked Harold Harris (21%) and will face each other in the November general election. The Metroparks replacement levy also passed with 52.4% in favor. • Also on Tuesday, Toledo City Council approved the city administration\'s Peace in Motion Plan to reduce gun violence and improve safety; approved $850,000 for fire and rescue vehicles and equipment and $250,000 toward for fire station improvements; and recognized the first week of May as National Small Business Week. • In further Tuesday news, Monclova-based agribusiness The Andersons Inc. reported pre-tax income of $3.2 million in the first quarter of 2025, down from $14 million in first quarter of 2024. President and CEO Bill Krueger cited "global trade uncertainties" for the "mixed" and "turbulent" numbers. • On Wednesday, Toledo-based building products manufacturer Owens Corning reported sales totaling $2.53 billion in the first quarter of 2025 compared to $2.017 billion during the same period in 2024. • On Thursday, the Toledo Plan Commission reviewed a study of tobacco shops which found that of 37 tobacco shops in the city, 13 are classified as legal, 3 as legal nonconforming, and 21 as illegal. The commission also recommended that city council approve changes to the zoning code for tobacco shops, including aligning the definition with state regulations to include vape products, requiring a license, and requiring them to be 1000 feet away from youth-related operations instead of the current 500 feet restriction. • Also on Thursday, the University of Toledo began demolition of Sullivan Hall as part of its Campus Master Plan. Two further buildings, Academic House and International House, will be demolished this summer and replaced with green space. The demolitions are expected to cost $2 million. • On Friday, a ribbon-cutting ceremony was held to unveil the new 262-unit Colony Lofts apartment complex on ProMedica Parkway. Rents in the high-end market rate complex range from $1,104 to $1,764 per month, plus utilities. • The Toledo Area Regional Transit Authority (TARTA) is now accepting applications for its Youth Summer Blast Pass, which allows students in Lucas County and Rossford ages 6 to 19 to take unlimited free bus rides from May 18 to August 31. For more information, visit https://tarta.com/blastapp/ • Councilwoman Theresa Morris has drafted legislation to bring back the ShotSpotter acoustic surveillance system in North Toledo and East Toledo. She is requesting $214,000 from the general fund for a version of ShotSpotter that has a smaller detection radius; previously, the program cost $840,000. • A recent study by J.D. Power found that Toledo water utility customers have the worst customer satisfaction scores of any midsize utility serving populations of 400,000 or more in the country. • Local PBS affiliate WGTE states that it is likely to lose $1.2 million - 21% of its annual budget - due to President Trump\'s executive order ceasing federal funding to PBS and NPR. The organization is looking for ways to cut its programming budget while also seeking other means of financial support to close the gap. • This Saturday (May 10) from 9 a.m. to 1 p.m., Engage Toledo will be hosting a drop off refuse and recycling event at Silver Creek Elementary School (2010 Northover Rd.). At no cost, individuals can drop off electronic waste, documents, cardboard, phone books, newspapers, houseware goods, clothing, toys, bulky items, appliances, latex paint, and refuse. Up to 10 tires can be recycled for $0.50 each. For more information, visit https://toledo.oh.gov/recycling-events. • Also this Saturday (May 10) from 9 a.m. to 4 p.m., Friends of the Library will be hosting a book sale featuring over 30,000 gently used books, CDs, DVDs, records, 8-track tapes, and board games at 1301 N. Reynolds Road. Each book is $1 or $8 for an entire bag. • Also this Saturday (May 10) from 10 a.m. to 2 p.m., Tree Toledo will be giving away free tree seedlings at Ohlmans Farm Market (3901 Hill Ave.). There is a limit of two trees per household, and species include Basswood, Black Gum, Hackberry, Sycamore, Norway Spruce, White Pine, and more. • Next Monday (May 12) from 6 p.m. to 7 p.m., the City of Toledo will host a public information session about the repair work on the two large sinkholes on Arlington Avenue, at Arlington Elementary (707 Woodsdale Ave.) • Next Thursday (May 15) at 6 p.m., a community meeting for South Toledo residents to discuss the ongoing reconstruction of Broadway Street at the Sofia Quintero Art and Cultural Center (1225 Broadway St.). City officials say construction should finish in July. • You can receive This Week in Toledo via e-mail by subscribing at https://toledo.substack.com/subscribe. You can also receive updates on Facebook by liking the official page at https://www.facebook.com/thisweekintoledo. News sources: The Blade, 13ABC, WTOL, Toledo Free Press', 'This is a long but important post. Anyone trying to feed you sound bites regarding what’s happening with Delmarva Power bills and energy costs in Delaware is doing you a major disservice and likely has ulterior motives. I encourage you to read this entire post and to share it. (Also, I urge you to review the important information regarding third-party energy suppliers towards the end of this post, as well as what Delmarva Power is doing to help consumers with their bills and other resources to help consumers with bills.) Last Friday, the Senate Energy Committee met and questioned Delmarva Power about recent spikes in Delmarva Power bills. The meeting lasted over three hours. I urge you to watch the recorded committee proceedings here. The committee will reconvene on Monday, February 24, at 1:00PM. I encourage you to watch the live proceedings or the recording. The recording will be posted at the same link as above. You can watch live proceedings by clicking on the appropriate link under “Live Proceedings" here. There is a false and dangerous narrative claiming that renewable energy and related Delaware legislation passed in recent years have driven Delmarva Power bill increases. That narrative is not based in facts and reality. First, some background. Established in 2009, Delaware’s Renewable Portfolio Standard (RPS) created a competitive market for utilities to acquire a certain percentage of their retail electricity from renewable sources like solar and wind energy. Those utilities can either generate electricity from renewable sources themselves or purchase credits from energy producers who have used renewable sources. If RPS legislation had never passed, consumers’ bills would likely be higher than they are today. Without the legislation, hundreds of megawatts of power being produced right now would not exist, meaning less energy in our grid and higher bills. According to the U.S. Energy Information Association, Delaware imports nearly 80 times the amount of energy that it produces. Democrats in the Delaware General Assembly have demonstrated a commitment to getting more energy projects online through multiple legislative initiatives targeting energy availability. Adding more renewable energy sources to our energy portfolio will bring down costs for Delawareans because we will need to import less energy. However, scarcity of renewable energy sources in Delaware is a direct result of both federal and Kent/Sussex county policies. We need renewable energy sources not just to combat climate change but also to lower energy costs for consumers. Such efforts include Sen. Stephanie Hansen’s 2021 Senate Bill (SB) 2, which created an infrastructure for Delmarva Power customers to participate in community solar projects, and SB 265, the “Delaware Energy Solutions Act of 2024,” which enabled the creation of a framework for the solicitation, consideration, and potential approval of future offshore wind projects. Some legislators and other individuals have pointed to SB 33 and how it "removed cost caps” for the RPS—which is very misleading. SB 33 removed the old mechanism for freezing the RPS, a mechanism that did not consider energy markets and offered no protections for consumers if energy prices increased. Then, SB 33 implemented a new cost cap considering energy markets, protecting consumers even if energy prices increase. It established that Delmarva Power would pay no more than $25 per renewable energy credit regardless of what the market does. SB 33 had the effect of lowering cost caps by over 75%. In a nutshell, Democratic legislators overhauled the cost cap to make things much better for consumers, not worse. Had SB 33 not passed, energy bills would be even higher than they are now. On average, Delmarva Power customers have seen their bills increase by about $8-$10 monthly because of legislation passed in recent years regarding renewable energy in Delaware. Those increases went into effect some time ago. That $8-10 has promoted the expansion of clean energy in Delaware and helped thousands of Delawareans install solar panels on their homes and businesses which has helped keep energy prices lower than what they would be without those clean energy investments. During committee proceedings, Chairwoman Sen. Hansen remarked, "To say that green energy is the culprit for Delmarva customers seeing astronomical energy bills this winter is wrong. When you look at the actual cause of the increase, you see that it’s driven by the increase in the amount of energy used, the increase in the cost of that energy, and the increase in the cost of the delivery of that energy. Trying to pin this on renewables is disingenuous. We know from our regional grid operator that we will be facing energy reliability problems within a few years if we do not do more to bring more renewable resources into our grid, and offshore wind plays a major role here.” Sen. Hansen continued, “We have to acknowledge that there are entities that have put up a big stop sign on renewable projects. We can’t say ‘woe is me’ without taking action to bring new energy projects online. Why don’t we say yes to offshore wind power? Why don’t we say yes to a solar farm in Kent County? We’re all on the same page about getting more electricity into the grid. Our dispute is about what kind of electricity is on the grid. It’s time to take down the stop signs on these critical projects.” Rep. Deb Heffernan, chairwoman of the House Energy Committee, remarked, “Skyrocketing energy bills are hitting families across our state hard, and they deserve to know what’s really driving these price increases. The reality is that renewable energy targets have been in place for years without causing major cost increases, and states without them—like Ohio—are also experiencing dramatic price spikes. " Taking an all-government approach, Sen. Hansen and Rep. Heffernan have been working with the Meyer Administration to develop a package of energy legislation that will address the issues of energy affordability, ratemaking, and other issues raised at the hearing. I thank these two legislators and the governor for putting in the hard work that will actually help bring down energy costs for Delawareans. Rep. Heffernan remarked, "I appreciate Delmarva for clearing up this misinformation and helping us get a better picture of what’s really going on with utility prices. Now, our focus is on solutions and getting real relief to the families who need it.” Regarding energy “usage,” the head of the Sierra Club of Delaware recently said: "There\'s a fundamental misunderstanding of what is meant by usage. After talking with some friends today, I think that folks are hearing that word and think that they actively did something to increase their usage. But that is usually not at all the case. Most people did not actively do anything to lead to high usage. "Now some folks did actively use more by using space heaters and what not. But it seems that for most people it was just passive heating costs, especially on the gas side. Again, there are no green mandates on gas. "If you didn\'t actively turn down your thermostat during that last winter blast, you got hit hard. If you didn\'t turn it down and used supplemental heat, it\'s a huge increase. And a dirty filter will cause a 15% increase in energy usage of any heating system, electric or gas. "The highest green energy-related charge I\'ve seen so far was around $20 on a $900 bill. Most of the bills I have seen with anything remotely green energy-related have been between $3-10 on bills between $350-500. "On the gas side, your delivery rate is double your supply rate. So with any temperatures below 20 degrees on the gas side, you start really stressing the system, using more gas to keep the same temperature, and then those delivery charges will kill you. But that\'s gas, not electric. Actively lowering your thermostat 2-3 degrees will drop your passive usage by about 50% for the day. "On the electric side, if you don\'t have a new high-efficiency cold climate heat pump, at around 25 degrees, if you don\'t turn down your thermostat, you likely kick into electric back-up heat. Basically a giant space heater for your entire house. "When it gets that cold and you have a whole house heat pump through central air ducts, the best thing you can do is set the thermostat 2-3 degrees lower than normal, shut the vents in rooms with no active thermostats that you are not using and focus the heat in the rooms with active thermostats and the rooms you use a lot. That is the best way to lower this passive usage when you\'re using a heat pump.” One big factor contributing to large increases in some consumers’ energy bills is that those consumers use a third-party supplier instead of Delmarva Power. In such cases, Delmarva Power is still your utility, meaning you use them for things like poles and wires. However, your actual energy supply comes from another company and Delmarva Power does not buy it off the market for you as it does for other consumers. Third-party suppliers can seem like a good deal at first, but consumers using these suppliers should watch their bills carefully. Third-party suppliers are probably not a good idea unless you are very knowledgeable about energy usage and how third-party suppliers work. Third-party suppliers often have hidden language in their contracts that negatively affect consumers, including high contract termination fees or monthly service fees. And third-party suppliers’ energy rates can soar dramatically, especially during very cold or very hot temperatures. Having a third-party supplier instead of Delmarva Power can be similar to having an adjustable-rate mortgage instead of a fixed-rate mortgage. Third-party vendors often use scam-like tactics to sign up customers. Recently, Delawareans (including myself) have received calls from third-party vendors claiming that they are partnering with Delmarva Power and that they are required by state government to contact Delaware households, neither of which is true. And they don’t honestly identify themselves or the purpose of their call. You can check your Delmarva Power bill to see if your energy supplier is Delmarva Power or a third-party vendor. Delmarva Power has put into place the following to help consumers with high bills. They are waiving late payment fees for January and February, suspending disconnections for non-payment in February 2025, and streamlining their process to restore service to disconnected customers. Delmarva Power has created this page that includes good information about various aspects of billing, higher bills, and how they can help customers with high energy bills: delmarva.com/billsupport. Customers can call 1-800-375-7117 to speak with a customer care representative directly. Also, here are resources for Delmarva Power customers needing assistance with paying high energy bills: Delaware 211, Catholic Charities, and Delaware State Service Centers. If you’ve read this entire post, thank you. I will continue to keep folks updated on this important topic. Please private message me with your email address if you’d like to sign up for my email blasts and newsletters.', 'How the State of Colorado Is Turning Its Back on Agriculture A Rancher’s Wake-Up Call The Van Winkle Ranch on Colorado’s Western Slope embodies four generations of agricultural stewardship. A recent article in Colorado Politics interviewed Janie Van Winkle about her perspective on the current political climate in the state. She says she’s contemplating selling her family’s ranch, something she once thought unimaginable. This statement should send shockwaves across the state and beyond the boundaries of agriculture. “The political environment is so toxic that if we do speak up at all, it falls on deaf ears,” Van Winkle said. “This is no longer an agriculture-friendly environment or atmosphere.” Her family’s story is not isolated. It is symbolic of a broader and dangerous trend in Colorado: the erosion of respect, support, and representation for agriculture and rural communities. Agriculture is Colorado’s Second-Largest Economic Driver: • Agriculture in Colorado contributes more than $47 billion annually to the economy and supports over 195,000 jobs. • Over 35,000 farms and ranches span 30.2 million acres, with an average farm size of 837 acres. • Cattle, corn, hay, dairy, and wheat are among the top commodities. • Exports of Colorado cattle alone reached $4.5 billion in 2024, placing the state in the top 10 nationally for beef exports. In Weld County, where AGPROfessionals is proud to be headquartered, agriculture remains the lifeblood: • Over $2.38 billion in commodity sales in 2022 • Nearly 875,000 acres of cropland • Still Colorado’s top ag-producing county, even amidst decline Yet despite these contributions, farmers and ranchers across the state are facing an avalanche of policy hostility, cultural dismissal, and political alienation. Urban-Rural Divide The growing divide between Colorado’s urban centers and rural regions is no longer a subtle rift. As highlighted in Colorado Politics’ Rural Reckoning series, rural counties feel underrepresented, underfunded, and under attack. Key points from Colorado Politics’ Rural Reckoning investigation: • Colorado has 53 rural counties, yet 11 urban counties dominate political influence. • Rural residents say Governor Polis governs Boulder and Denver, not for them. • Rewilding, anti-ag ballot initiatives, infrastructure neglect, and wildlife policies are deepening the rift. A Pattern of Policy Undermining Agriculture The last several years have brought a relentless stream of initiatives and appointments that have undermined Colorado’s agricultural community. Below is a list of just some of the issues and political initiatives Colorado farmers and ranchers have been facing in recent years: • Colorado House Bill 21-1286: the Energy Performance for Buildings Statute, was established with the goal of helping building owners increase energy efficiency, lower energy costs, and decrease greenhouse gas emissions. It requires buildings over 50,000 square feet to comply with new energy standards by 2030. According to the law, agricultural buildings are exempt. However, via policy and procedure at the state level, that was not the case. In this situation, AGPROfessionals were able to work with lawmakers for new language that made it clear that agricultural buildings were truly exempt. • Governor Polis Promotes the Impossible Burger: In 2019, the governor urged the Colorado Department of Agriculture to promote the Impossible Burger. It is important to note that the founders of Impossible have publicly stated their aim is to put animal agriculture out of business. • "MeatOut Day" Proclamation: In 2021, Gov. Polis encouraged Coloradans to abstain from eating meat — in a state built on cattle, sheep, and stewardship. The message to producers? You\'re not part of Colorado’s future. • Ballot Initiative IP 16: The proposal “Protect Animals from Unnecessary Suffering and Exploitation (PAUSE)” sought to amend Colorado laws to criminalize certain animal husbandry, reproductive, and medical practices, change slaughter ages, and remove cruelty law exemptions. Submitted in February 2021 for the November 2022 election, it was listed as Initiative 16 on March 17, 2021. Opponents challenged the title in court, arguing it violated the single-subject rule. Fortunately, it did not make it onto the ballot, but the fact that it was approved and listed in the first place is cause for concern. • Appointment of Animal Rights Ideologue to the Colorado State Veterinary Board: Appointed to the Colorado State Veterinary Board, Ellen Kessler, an animal rights activist, publicly called 4-H clubs "child abuse," referred to ranchers as "lazy and nasty," and was later charged with 13 counts of animal cruelty and resigned from the board. • Colorado House Bill 21-1286: the Energy Performance for Buildings Statute, was established with the goal of helping building owners increase energy efficiency, lower energy costs, and decrease greenhouse gas emissions. It requires buildings over 50,000 square feet to comply with new energy standards by 2030. According to the law, agricultural buildings are exempt. However, via policy and procedure at the state level, that was not the case. In this situation, AGPROfessionals were able to work with lawmakers for new language that made it clear, agricultural buildings were truly exempt. • Wolf Reintroduction, Calf Killing Sprees and Land Threats: After narrowly passing in urban precincts, the wolf reintroduction program is wreaking havoc on ranchlands. Even more disturbing, a video circulating shows that state officials recently discussed the wolf issue, and one of the panelists advocated for protecting the wolves over farmers, ranchers, and private property owners. He suggested first offering to buy out ranchers who would sell their land “voluntarily” to support wolves, and then taking private lands from the remaining ranchers via eminent domain if necessary. • Ballot Initiative #82: The Colorado Wildlife & Biodiversity Protection Act: If voted into law, this extreme measure would strip authority from Colorado Parks and Wildlife. Most glaringly it would create a new unelected commission with unchecked power, explicitly leaving farmers, ranchers, and others involved in agriculture off the commission. We outlined these issues and more in an article on our website (see link below). • Denver Ballot Initiative to Ban Processing Facilities in the City: A recent ballot initiative in Denver, Initiated Ordinance 309, also known as the slaughterhouse ban, aimed to prohibit the construction, maintenance, or use of slaughterhouses within the city limits. Voters rejected the slaughterhouse ban (Ordinance 309) on election day. Nearly 65% of ballots were cast against the measure. • Denver Ballot Initiative to Ban the Sale of Fur Products: Initiated Ordinance 308, sought to ban the sale of fur products, which would have also banned the production and sale of cowboy hats in the city. • Appointment of Rewilding Advocate to The Colorado State Land Board: Governor Polis appointed Nicole Rosmarino, a self-described rewilding advocate, as director of the State Land Board. Prior to her appointment, the Non-Governmental Organizations she worked for openly sought to “reclaim” ranchland for wolves and remove livestock from public lands. It is important to note that the land board oversees 2.8 million acres of state trust land, and Rosmarino’s vision conflicts directly with the constitutional mandate to generate revenue from state lands for Colorado’s school system. • $3 Million of Denver Budget Dedicated to Anti-Meat Campaign: The City of Denver has allocated a $3 million budget for a marketing campaign promoting plant-based diets as a strategy to combat climate change. Colorado representative Ty Miller along with a number of other rural state representatives oppose this use of taxpayer funds, stating, “This initiative sends the wrong message and disrespects the ranchers and farmers who put food on Colorado tables.” • Wolf Mandate Ballot Initiative Rejected: The Title Board sided with opponents who claimed Initiative #35 violated the state\'s single-subject rule. According to an article in Colorado Politics, Patrick Davis campaign manager stated, "With this decision, the Title Board is denying the owners of livestock guard and herding animals the chance for compensation for losses caused by a gray wolf and denying the professionals at the Colorado Department of Parks and Wildlife the chance to be given flexibility to manage Colorado’s wolf population as they see fit,” Additionally, Colorado voters were denied the opportunity to weigh in on prohibiting wolves from being imported into Colorado." Farmers and Ranchers Are Not the Enemy Whether it\'s wolves, water, or climate, agriculture is increasingly seen as a problem to be solved, not a partner. The truth is that agriculture provides for our every need, from food to fuel, fiber, and pharmaceuticals, yet this reality continues to be ignored. This narrative is not just wrong; it’s harmful. Its roots are in the United Nations’ FAO report, Livestock’s Long Shadow, which was disproved by Dr. Frank Mitloehner of UC Davis. He demonstrated that animal agriculture in the United States accounts for only a small part of greenhouse gas emissions. Even after the FAO was forced to withdraw its findings, the ideological attack on agriculture persists. Where Do We Go from Here? At AGPROfessionals, our roots began in Weld County, Colorado. Unfortunately, we have observed a troubling shift in Colorado over the past eight years. Once the backbone of state policy and values, agriculture now faces escalating ideological and bureaucratic challenges. We call on state leaders to recognize the contributions agriculture makes to the state of Colorado and to uphold constitutional mandates: • Return Governance to the People: Appointments to boards governing agriculture, wildlife, and land use must reflect individuals grounded in rural realities and agricultural best practices, not ideologically driven outsiders. Board and commission appointments impacting land use should include experienced farmers and ranchers. • Protect Private Property Rights: No ballot initiative or agency should have the power to trample private property rights, or destroy lawful businesses, especially for ideological agendas that destroy rural economies and the backbone of our state. • Rebuild the Partnership: Colorado cannot thrive by ignoring and trampling on the rights of the people who feed, clothe, and power it. Meaningful engagement with rural communities requires legislators and Capitol leaders who understand agricultural economics, water policy, fire mitigation, and food security priorities. Janie Van Winkle’s pause to consider selling her family ranch signals more than personal fatigue. It illustrates the risk of losing Colorado’s agricultural soul. The growing divide between urban ideologies and rural production is no accident. It comes from decisions that bypass working landowners, misalign governance appointments, and insert ideological agendas into land, wildlife, and food policy. Links to references and articles can be found in this article on our website #colorado #coloradoagriculture #agriculture #policy #polis #GovernorPolis #government #ideologicalagenda #dairy #farming #ranching #beefproduction #feedingfamilies #stateeconomy #agproud #livestockproduction #animalagriculture']

Representative_Docs: ["𝐂𝐨𝐦𝐦𝐢𝐬𝐬𝐢𝐨𝐧𝐞𝐫 𝐍𝐢𝐜𝐨𝐥𝐞 𝐖𝐢𝐥𝐬𝐨𝐧—𝐀 𝐂𝐡𝐚𝐦𝐩𝐢𝐨𝐧 𝐟𝐨𝐫 𝐎𝐮𝐫 𝐄𝐧𝐯𝐢𝐫𝐨𝐧𝐦𝐞𝐧𝐭 𝐚𝐧𝐝 𝐅𝐮𝐭𝐮𝐫𝐞 𝐆𝐞𝐧𝐞𝐫𝐚𝐭𝐢𝐨𝐧𝐬 In a time when our environment faces unprecedented challenges, it is essential to have leaders who prioritize sustainability and the protection of our natural resources. Commissioner Nicole Wilson has consistently demonstrated her unwavering commitment to safeguarding our environment, ensuring that future generations inherit a West Orange County that thrives both ecologically and economically. One of Commissioner Wilson’s standout achievements is the adoption of the Fertilizer Ordinance. This crucial policy limits nutrient pollution, which is a significant contributor to the degradation of our waterways. By addressing this issue head-on, Commissioner Wilson has taken a proactive step toward preserving the health of our lakes, rivers, and streams. This ordinance isn’t just about maintaining water quality today—it’s about ensuring that our children and grandchildren can enjoy the same clean, vibrant water bodies that we do. Commissioner Wilson also recognizes the importance of modernizing our infrastructure to protect our environment. Through her leadership, essential Septic-to-Sewer Conversions have been funded, which play a vital role in maintaining clean water standards. This initiative not only improves the quality of our drinking water but also reduces the risk of harmful pollutants seeping into our environment. It's a long-term investment in the health and safety of our community, showing that Commissioner Wilson is planning for the future, not just managing the present. Wetlands are among the most biologically diverse and important ecosystems, yet they are often the most vulnerable. Commissioner Wilson’s Wetland Conservation Ordinance exemplifies her commitment to protecting these vital areas. By championing better protection and wider buffers for wetlands, she has ensured that these lands continue to provide critical habitat for wildlife, serve as natural water filters, and help mitigate the impacts of flooding. This ordinance is a testament to her understanding that our environment is an intricate system where every element plays a crucial role. Trees are often called the lungs of our planet, and Commissioner Wilson has gone above and beyond to ensure their protection. The Tree Protection Ordinance she strengthened promotes species diversity and mandates that developers replace the tree canopy at higher rates. This ordinance has not gone unnoticed; it has been recognized by the Florida Urban Forestry Council and the Arbor Day Foundation. Such recognition highlights the significance of her efforts and the positive impact they have on our community. By preserving our trees, Commissioner Wilson is not only beautifying our county but also fighting climate change, improving air quality, and enhancing the overall well-being of our residents. Commissioner Nicole Wilson’s environmental initiatives are not just policies on paper; they are actionable steps that have already begun to make a difference in our community. Her leadership has set a standard for what it means to care for our environment, and her dedication to this cause is evident in every ordinance she supports and every project she funds. As Election Day approaches on August 20, it is crucial that we recognize the importance of re-electing a leader who has proven her commitment to our community. Commissioner Nicole Wilson has shown that she not only listens to the needs of her constituents but also takes decisive action to address those needs. Her vision for a sustainable and thriving West Orange County is one that we should all support. Make your voice heard by voting early or on Election Day to re-elect Commissioner Nicole Wilson. Her proven leadership and dedication ensure a brighter, more sustainable future for all of us. Together, we can continue to build a better West Orange County—one that our children and grandchildren will be proud to call home. Visit votenicolewilson.com Let’s work together to keep our community strong, safe, and sustainable. #OrangeCounty #Orlando #District1 #VoteNicoleWilson #WilsonForCommissioner #CommunityFirst #EnvironmentalProtection #PublicSafety #CommunityEngagement #ReElectNicoleWilson", '‘Forever chemicals’ have cost Merrimack millions; now leaders seek fair share of settlement funds By: Claire Sullivan Merrimack has paid a steep price to clean up PFAS pollution in its public drinking water. A lawmaker said Friday she hopes that doesn’t mean the town will miss out on funds from massive lawsuit settlements from manufacturers of the “forever chemicals.” Rep. Rosemarie Rung, a Merrimack Democrat, pointed out that in New Hampshire those settlement funds will be directed to the Drinking Water and Groundwater Trust Fund, which will provide loans and grants to public water systems where levels of PFAS, a class of harmful man-made chemicals, are above standards. In 2019, voters approved the Merrimack Village District to spend $14.5 million to filter four public wells contaminated with PFAS. Saint-Gobain Performance Plastics, a French manufacturer that emitted PFAS from its Merrimack facility, agreed to foot filtration for two other public wells. “I think the fair thing to do would be that Merrimack Village District should be able to get money … from that fund to cover that cost that the ratepayers committed to years ago,” Rung said. “You know, we’ve set the example about how to get out there and address a problem head on and don’t wait, and take the leadership, and it seems as though that’s not being acknowledged.” Rung hopes those distributing settlement funds will “be fair to us and use some of that money to cover that cost.” The state is expected to get upwards of $65 million in the next two to three years as the result of settlements with manufacturers of PFAS and aqueous film-forming foam, also known as AFFF. The money was one of many issues addressed at a Friday morning press conference of Democratic lawmakers and citizen activists concerned by PFAS pollution in the state. They celebrated wins in the legislative session but also acknowledged the long path ahead to root out the chemicals that break down at an extraordinarily slow pace. PFAS are linked to numerous health problems – high cholesterol, weakened immune systems, some cancers, and more – and can be found in almost all humans. The scene of the press conference, a Merrimack park along the Souhegan River, illustrated that even in a state revered for its natural beauty, the harmful, man-made chemicals are ubiquitous. Beneath a canopy of trees, sunlight streaming through the wet leaves, lawmakers and residents held signs with slogans like “people over polluters” and “stop the source.” “Look around at this beautiful, beautiful, beautiful town,” said Laurene Allen, co-founder of Merrimack Citizens for Clean Water. “We have many, many parks, we have many, many natural resources, and who would guess that it’s all contaminated with the most persistent of all endocrine disruptors, PFAS?” One bill passed this session aims to stem the flow of PFAS-laden products into the state. House Bill 1649, approved by Gov. Chris Sununu in August, will ban certain products with intentionally added PFAS from sale in the state starting in 2027. The chemicals are often found in everyday products, used to make things stain- or water-resistant. The ban will include cosmetics, carpets, feminine hygiene products, products for young children, and more. The use of the chemicals in so many corners of society has spread the contamination far and wide. “Constantly, PFAS are being discovered where they shouldn’t be: food, drinking water, lakes and rivers, soil, freshwater fish, seafood, ocean spray, breast milk, and even the blood of newborns,” said Rep. Karen Ebel, a New London Democrat who was the prime sponsor of the bill. “Why? Because these harmful ‘forever chemicals’ are used in just about everything you can imagine.” Though Sununu approved the product ban, which also created strict liability for PFAS polluters, he shot down House Bill 1415, which would’ve created liability for PFAS facilities and had a much stricter standard than the bill he signed. He cited concerns from the New Hampshire Business and Industry Association over the conflicting standards. The prime sponsor of that bill, Rep. Nancy Murphy, a Merrimack Democrat, said there would be an attempt to override the veto when lawmakers convene Oct. 10. “I will likely refile HB 1415 and hope that our next governor will stand up for New Hampshire citizens and prioritize people and planet over polluter profits,” she said. Many of those at the press conference Friday spoke to the issue not just in political terms, but personal ones. Rep. Wendy Thomas, a Merrimack Democrat who got a bill passed this session to notify property-buyers about possible PFAS pollution, has breast cancer she attributes to the high levels of PFAS chemicals detected in her blood. Her husband had a quadruple bypass at age 55. She said dogs in town are put down “all the time because of cancer.” “This stuff is very, very bad,” she said. “If you consume it long enough, you’re going to get sick.” @[100058187404164:2048:Rosemarie Rung, NH State Representative]@[100057974473497:2048:Nancy Murphy, NH State Representative]@[100057790340007:2048:Wendy E. N Thomas, NH State Representative]@[100003045336741:2048:Laurene Allen]@[100058219863319:2048:Karen Ebel - NH State Representative]Claire Sullivan https://newhampshirebulletin.com/2024/09/30/forever-chemicals-have-cost-merrimack-millions-now-leaders-seek-fair-share-of-settlement-funds/?emci=a591599c-0f7d-ef11-8474-6045bda8aae9&emdi=b4230523-1b7f-ef11-8474-6045bda8aae9&ceid=150956', 'If you haven\'t been able to attend any of the ongoing in-person presentations regarding the wastewater project being conducted by the @[100064556145400:2048:Town of Dennis], here is a video of that presentation. Don\'t forget - Dennis Town Meeting is October 1st! #OneWaterDennis ############################## The following is a transcription of the slides to accompany the voice over from Dennis wastewater project coordinator Marc Drainville. ############################## Dennis Wastewater Project 2024 – Wastewater Wednesday Workshop Online Public Presentation 1. Workshop Agenda - Water Resource Recovery Facility (WRRF) - Phase 1 Collection System - Watershed Permitting - Additional Project Permitting - Project Funding Key Dates - Project Funding Elements - Cost Recovery Sources - Financing Model - Financing Plan – "The Dennis Plan" ### 2. Water Resource Recovery Facility (WRRF) - Wastewater Resource Recovery Facility - Design has been completed. - Contractor preselection has been completed and notifications sent on June 13th. - Current Bidding Schedule - Advertisement: June 26th (DONE) - Pre-bid meeting: July 18th (DONE) - Sub-bid opening: August 13th (DONE) - General bid opening: September 12th - Contract No. 1 – "The Plant" - Bidding schedule ensures Dennis can go to Town Meeting with bids in hand. ### 3. Wastewater Collection System - Collection System – Overview - 12.5 miles of gravity sewers - 4.5 miles of pressure sewers - 8 pumping stations - 3.4 miles of force main - 3 construction contracts - Collection System – Contracts - Contract No. 2 - 25,000 linear feet of gravity sewer - 6,100 linear feet of pressure sewer - 4 pumping stations - 11,900 linear feet of force main - Contract No. 3 - 25,200 linear feet of gravity sewer - 1,200 linear feet of pressure sewer - 1 pumping station - 2,800 linear feet of force main - Contract No. 4 - 14,800 linear feet of gravity sewer - 16,800 linear feet of pressure sewer - 3 pumping stations - 3,000 linear feet of force main - Contract No. 1 is the WRRF – "The Plant." ### 4. Collection System Status - Progress - Contract Nos. 2, 3 & 4 moving toward 100% submittal. - Geotechnical Drilling Program completed. - Pumping station locations confirmed. - MHC – Intensive investigation of pumping station site completed. - MassDOT Highway Access Permit ongoing. - Conservation Commission submittals completed (both Harwich & Dennis). - Next Steps - Contract Nos. 2, 3 & 4 moving to start bidding (Middle of August). - Bid openings in mid-September, staggered one week apart. ### 5. Wastewater Project Permitting - Dennis Watershed Permits - In July 2023, the State of Massachusetts instituted Watershed Permits for nitrogen-impaired estuaries. - 20-year permits provide the framework for returning the estuaries to a healthy state. - The Town of Dennis is committed to complying with these permits by implementing the wastewater infrastructure program. - Compliance will help restore the environment and avoid penalties. - Dennis\' Permitting Progress - The Dennis wastewater team is ensuring all necessary permits, both locally and from the State, are in place. - Milestones Achieved: - All necessary permits are on track, with successful orders of conditions from both the Dennis and Harwich Conservation Commissions. - Collaboration with DEP & DWD: - Regular meetings ensure securing the critical groundwater discharge permit for the WRRF and Phase 1 infrastructure. - Project Oversight by Massachusetts DEP - DEP Involvement: - Multiple divisions of DEP, DWD, and the Town of Dennis are overseeing the project to ensure it meets all environmental and public health standards. - Continuous Monitoring: - DEP’s regular and active role ensures a holistic view of the clean water project, safeguarding the community’s health and all water resources. ### 6. Wastewater Project Financing - Historic Funding Opportunity Secured - Dennis is in the top tier of the State Revolving Fund (SRF) for this project. - MA Nitrogen Priority: - Dennis can access vital infrastructure funding due to the State’s prioritization of nitrogen-sensitive clean water projects. - Significant Savings: - Zero percent [0%] interest and over 30% principal forgiveness through programs like SRF and the Clean Water Protection Fund. - The Dennis Plan - The Town of Dennis’ cost recovery team developed a sustainable and equitable financial model tailored to residents\' needs. - Phase 1 Construction Cost Overview: - $248.5 million in construction costs - $151.7 million for treatment plant - $96.8 million for the collection system - Process: - Engineering firms provided source data for construction costs, which were aggregated with debt data and projections by the municipal finance consultant. - Inter-department meetings resulted in Dennis’ Phase 1 parcel list, defining the project scope and financial model. - Diverse Cost Recovery Resources - Q: How is Dennis easing the burden on taxpayers? - A: By leveraging a wide range of funding sources, including SRF, principal forgiveness, WIIF, local option rooms tax, solar generation revenue, and short-term rental fees. - Lowering the Impact: - A diversified approach ensures less burden on residents and maximizes financial sustainability. - Minimal Tax Impact, Maximum Community Health - Tax Impact: - A modest increase of $0.49 per $1,000 valuation—approximately $400 annually for the average homeowner. - Long-Term Benefits: - This small investment ensures a healthier, more sustainable Dennis for generations to come. - Example Math: If your home is assessed at $1,000,000, you would pay less than $500 annually. *Additional applied grant funding may further reduce this amount. ### Final Thoughts - This Is Not a Sewer Project - It is a wastewater reclamation initiative that fulfills Dennis\' commitment to preserving natural resources. By reclaiming, recycling, and reusing water, the project protects waterways and beaches, supports the seasonal economy, preserves property values, and safeguards the health and longevity of residents. - Town Meeting – October 1st']

Representative_Docs: ['Good morning everyone! I want to take a minute to re-introduce myself to new followers and to introduce myself to anyone I may not have met as of today. I am a lifelong Floyd Countian. My parents met while working for the FBI. They were married and decided that Floyd County was a great place to settle down and raise their family. I grew up in Garden Lakes and graduated from Coosa High School. After I graduated from high school, I attended Augusta State College and received a degree in Biology. After graduating from college, our love for Rome, Floyd County, and Cave Spring led Beth and I back here. Beth, the love of my life, and I have been married for over 30 years. We knew that Rome, Floyd, and Cave Spring was the place we wanted to plant our roots and establish a family. Upon our return home, I started a landscaping business, and I am proud to say that ForEvergreen has proudly served the Rome, Floyd, and Cave Spring area since 1998. Beth and I have 3 great children: Noah, Braden, Katelyn. We have proudly raised our children in a Christian home. We attend the 1st Baptist Church of Rome. I am a Deacon there and Beth is deeply involved there also. We have made sure that our children were involved and active in Sunday School and the youth program. Each year Beth and I take the youth to the church’s annual ski trip, and we help out with the numerous 1st Baptist Rome fundraisers. In addition to church, our kids have been active in academics and athletics at their respective schools. We take pride in saying we have raised well round children. We have taught them to love their family, and we have taught them the value of hard work and giving back to their community. From an early age, I learned the importance of a strong work ethic and the value of giving back to my community. As a young man, I got up early in the mornings and rode my bicycle around Garden Lakes delivering Rome News Tribune newspapers. In the afternoons of those same days, I worked cutting grass around Garden Lakes. During this time, I saved almost every penny I earned to buy my 1st car. These early mornings were sometimes pretty cold or afternoons that were pretty hot.. But they helped form the core of hard work and determination that I still have today. When I returned to Rome, Floyd County, and Cave Spring after college, I founded ForeverGreen Landscaping. My business employs 30 great people. I am very proud to say ForeverGreen has survived through recessions in the economy, housing booms and downturns, a pandemic, and other events that have affected the cost of equipment, fuel, energy, and personnel. As a small business owner, I understand the importance of fiscal responsibility, hard work, and the challenges of balancing many financial decisions. I have always been passionate about serving others, being plugged-in, and being involved. I have served actively in numerous local organizations. * The Boys and Girl’s Club Board * Northwest GA Sexual Assault Board * A contributing member of the Rome-Floyd Chamber of Commerce * Leader of 2 Rome/Floyd Chamber of * Commerce Leadership Rome classes * Sarah Hightower Library Board * Floyd County Airport Commission * Elected to the Floyd County Board of Education I am passionate about continuing to make Rome, Floyd County, and Cave Spring a better place for everyone. I believe my experience as a small business owner, my commitment to service, and my leadership on various boards and commissions have prepared me to tackle the challenges facing Rome, Floyd County, and Cave Spring. These positions have also prepared me to represent the people of Rome, Floyd County, and Cave Spring with integrity and dedication with a focus on the future. I am running for the Floyd County Commission, Post 4, because I believe it is the right time to step up and give back in a new way. I had a discussion with the late commissioner Larry Maxey about his plans for retirement and about his support should I one day choose to run a campaign for the County Commission. Unfortunately, Larry did not get to see the day when he would be able to pass the gavel to the next generation. However, I am privileged and honored to have the endorsement of Larry’s wife, Patricia Maxey. My involvement in our local government through committees and boards, my involvement overseeing multi-million dollar budgets, my overseeing personnel decisions for groups ranging from 30 – 1000+, my involvement in government policy making, my involvement in State and Federal certification processes for groups ranging from the airport to the school system have all prepared me to serve on the Floyd County Commission. I will be ready on Day 1 to take the Post 4 seat. I will work along with the other Commissioners for the betterment of Rome, Floyd County, and Cave Spring. However, I WILL BE your voice. I WILL work for you. I WILL seek your input and guidance on issues that affect your every day lives. I WILL stand up for what is right. I WILL protect your tax dollars. I WILL fight for your families. I WILL lead with honesty and integrity. I WILL be a voice for EVERYONE and EVERY group. I appreciate the time you have taken to learn about me from my early upbringing by my wonderful parents through Beth and I raising our own children. My hope is that you have learned about my work background/ethic and see something similar to your own. My hope is you see my involvement and experience with various organizations, committees, and boards as something that would bring much value to the Floyd County Commission. My hope is that you will feel my experience with personnel decisions for organizations that have 30 – 1,000+ employees, experience with budgets dealing with multi-millions of dollars, my experience dealing with Federal and State matters are ALL something that will add value to the Floyd County Commission. I thank you for your time, Chip #RomeGA #FloydCountyGA #CaveSpringGA', "I am excited to announce my candidacy for re-election as Mayor of the City of Guntersville. Having spent my entire life in this wonderful community, I am blessed to call it home. I’m committed to continuing my service to the people of Guntersville—my friends, family, and neighbors—by ensuring the continued growth and prosperity of the city we all love. Over the past 13 years, we've made significant strides together, but there is still more to be done. I am eager to keep working with the outstanding team at the City to build on the progress we've already made and move our community forward. Guntersville is a truly special place—a great place to live, work, and play. But maintaining and growing this quality of life doesn’t happen by chance. It takes strong management, financial stewardship, and a forward-thinking vision. As your Mayor, I will continue to focus on what’s best for our community and work to preserve our city’s excellence for both residents and visitors. Guntersville’s unique geographical location gives us a distinct advantage for growth, and we’re already seeing the positive impact of that growth. But the potential is even greater, and much of the groundwork has been laid to leverage that potential. I’m committed to continuing this work by promoting smart, sustainable development, with an emphasis on tourism and recreation as well as on commercial and industrial development. Our city is full of opportunities, many of which are already turning into real accomplishments every day. I’m proud of the role that positive leadership has played in those successes—from fostering strong relationships with the City Council to creating a culture of teamwork and ownership within our city staff. The results speak for themselves. As Mayor, one of my top priorities has been to protect and optimize Guntersville’s natural resources. This includes not only maintaining infrastructure but also enhancing our resources to support responsible growth. Under my leadership, we’ve made great progress in both areas. We’ve paved nearly every street in the city, attracted major retailers, and overseen the development of the City Harbor. We have also virtually filled Conners Island Business Park, including the exciting JST project now underway for a world-class manufacturing and design facility. We are nearing completion of extensive renovations and upgrades at the majority of the recreation facilities. We have completed the “under the bridge” walking trail, added a splash pad at the pool complex, and made major improvements at piers and boat ramps throughout the City. From a facility and infrastructure standpoint, we built a new recycling center, and built a new high school. But there’s still more to come, including the addition of Fire Station #3, continued renovations to the remaining recreation facilities, and safety improvements at key intersections. Education is critical to the well-being of our city, and as Mayor, I’m proud of the collaborative efforts between the City and the School Board in the completion of the new high school. It’s been a true team effort, and I look forward to continuing that partnership to make Guntersville’s school system one of the best in the state. As a CPA, strong financial management has always been a priority for me. Thanks to our disciplined approach, Guntersville has maintained clean audits, solid budgets, and a financial position that many other cities envy. I am proud to report that the city’s finances remain in great shape. If re-elected I will continue to be a responsible steward of the citizens' resources, and keep Guntersville on a firm financial footing for years to come. I believe that teamwork is the key to our success. My work as Mayor is a reflection of the fantastic team of employees, volunteers, and leaders in this community. I remain accessible to everyone, always willing to listen, and committed to considering new ideas. Whether it’s a suggestion, a respectful concern, or an innovative idea, I believe that together, we can make Guntersville an even better place. I have been honored to serve as your Mayor for the past three terms, and I am incredibly grateful for the support you’ve shown me. I appreciate your trust. It has been my privilege to serve, and I humbly ask for your vote and continued support for four more years of progress. Let’s continue to build on the success and make Guntersville an even greater place to call home.", '🚨 ANNOUNCING THE MMK SLATE FOR TANEYTOWN CITY COUNCIL🚨 --------- Be sure to check out the candidate pages as well: https://www.facebook.com/profile.php?id=61570362497549 (Harry) https://www.facebook.com/rm4tt/ (Rachael) https://www.facebook.com/profile.php?id=61570114247481 (Nick) --------- Taneytown Residents, The MMK Slate is proud to introduce a bold vision for our city—one rooted in transparency, collaboration, and a commitment to putting YOU, the residents of Taneytown, first. With a clear plan of action and policy initiatives ready to roll out on Day One, we are prepared to hit the ground running the moment we are sworn in. This campaign is about redefining how Taneytown’s government operates, ensuring a balanced and efficient government that prioritizes clear boundaries and collaboration between the Mayor and Council. Our goal is to establish a system where each branch focuses on its unique responsibilities, supplements one another, and works together to better serve the residents. What follows is just a small portion of the platform we’ve built to address the challenges and opportunities ahead for Taneytown. More details will be released throughout the campaign to keep residents informed and engaged. ✨ Our mission is simple yet ambitious: ✔️ Preserve and enhance Taneytown’s quality of life while retaining its small-town charm. ✔️ Address critical areas like commercial development, public safety, government responsiveness, and transparency. ✔️ Redefine government roles to ensure the Mayor and Council work efficiently within their unique responsibilities, fostering collaboration for the residents’ benefit. ✔️ Deliver action, not just promises, starting on Day One. --------- 🌟 Meet the MMK Slate Candidates 🌟 💼 Harry Meade (M) 🛠️ Roots in Carroll County: Resident since 1986, with a successful career in commercial audio-visual systems, rising to lead designer on a Federal Contract. 🎶 Cultural Advocate: Lifelong bagpipe band participant, competing internationally in prestigious events like the Rose Parade and World Championships. ✨ Vision: Committed to transparency, community involvement, and preserving Taneytown’s history and small-town feel while driving actionable change. 💼 Rachael Miller (M) 🎓 Professional Expertise: Holds a Master’s in Homeland Security and a Bachelor’s in Computer Science. Federal employee specializing in administration, budgeting, and contracts. Co-owner of Laughing Coffin, a local hobby shop and e-sports center. 👩\u200d👧\u200d👦 Family and Roots: Mother of five and dedicated community advocate who has embraced Taneytown as her permanent home. ✨ Vision: Focused on building a government that prioritizes residents, improves services, and creates opportunities while preserving Taneytown’s charm. 💼 Nick Kalinock (K) 🎓 Educational Background: Holds a Bachelor’s in Economics and a Master’s in Finance from the University of Maryland, with expertise in fiscal responsibility and strategic decision-making. 🌍 Professional Leadership: Nick is a seasoned financial leader with extensive experience in delivering high-stakes production solutions. As a Director at Morgan Stanley in Baltimore, Maryland, Nick manages a team of four professionals and interfaces with teams around the world to ensure seamless operations and exceptional outcomes. His leadership emphasizes precision, efficiency, and innovation, and he is highly regarded by both his colleagues and the people he leads, reflecting a career dedicated to driving excellence in the financial industry. ✨ Vision: Dedicated to transparent leadership, responsible urban planning, and ensuring sustainable growth for Taneytown. --------- 🌟 The MMK Platform: A Glimpse Into the Goals🌟 🏛️ City Government Goals ✔️ Reorganize Boards and Committees to focus on impactful outcomes, including re-establishing the Main Street Committee to support businesses and attract residents. ✔️ Charter and Code Overhaul for clarity and modernization. ✔️ Term Limits: Limit the Mayor to 8 years and Council members to 12 years, ensuring fresh perspectives. ✔️ Defined Roles: Empower the Council to focus Add sports fields and create a Community Center to serve as a hub for nonprofit services and recreation. 💼 Economic Development Initiatives ✔️ Promote citywide business growth and unify the business community to better serve all residents. 🚧 Public Works Priorities ✔️ Add speed measures, neighborhood lighting, and signage for safer streets. ✔️ Establish a Water Relief Fund for families in need. ✔️ Partner with nonprofits and youth organizations to assist elderly residents. --------- 🗳️ Why Vote for MMK on May 5, 2025? The MMK Slate is ready to lead from Day One, implementing policies and reforms that prioritize residents above all else. By voting for the MMK Slate, you are voting for: ✅ A government that works together to serve the community. ✅ Transparent leadership with clearly defined roles for the Mayor and Council. ✅ Policies that enhance public safety, promote responsible growth, and improve quality of life. Together, we can make Taneytown a better place for generations to come. Cast your vote for Meade, Miller, and Kalinock on May 5, 2025, and help us build a Taneytown that works for YOU.']

Representative_Docs: ['Please sign the petition to End Harmful Dairy Subsidies: www.Change.org/Got-Milked 🔥 California is getting burned: We shouldn\'t be subsidizing an industry that produces half this state\'s methane emissions, and when dairy milk produces almost 3× the greenhouse gas emissions of any plant-milk. 💪 Did TV ads lead you to believe that we need cow\'s milk? Listen to these Olympic athletes who switched: www.WhyPlantMilk.info/#lv-switch4good-sh 💧 In California, animal ag uses about 45% of our state\'s water, while households use 5%. Just 1 gallon of milk requires over 500 gallons of water to produce, while the average hamburger requires over 600 gallons. Remember that whenever media is drenching you with drought+fire fears followed by fast food and dairy commercials. 🌲 The Amazon rainforest (an important oxygen-producing resource for all Earthlings) is burning because of cattle: https://www.NYTimes.com/video/world/americas/100000006721982/amazon-rainforest-fires-burning.html 🌏 Oxford University researchers found that plant-based could be #1 way to reduce our environmental impact ― far bigger than buying an electric car: www.WhyPlantMilk.info/#lt-environment-sh 🍔 "We are, quite literally, gambling with the future of our planet, for the sake of hamburgers [and cow babies\' milk]." ― Peter Singer, Professor of Bioethics at Princeton University 🍼 Cow\'s milk is designed specifically for cow babies, and only industrial-sized white lies convinced us otherwise: www.WhyPlantMilk.info/#lt-whitelies-sh REFERENCES: • California dairy subsidies: www.TheGuardian.com/environment/2022/feb/04/california-subsidies-biogas-dairy-cows-emissions-climate • Letter to Governor Gavin Newsom: www.PETA.org/media/news-releases/cows-milk-or-california-peta-holds-newsoms-feet-to-the-fire-demands-end-to-animal-ag-subsidies-burning-california/ • University of Oxford: www.Independent.co.uk/life-style/health-and-families/veganism-environmental-impact-planet-reduced-plant-based-diet-humans-study-a8378631.html • United Nations on global warming: https://news.UN.org/en/story/2006/11/201222-rearing-cattle-produces-more-greenhouse-gases-driving-cars-un-report-warns • BBC on the most planet-friendly dairy alternatives: www.BBC.com/news/science-environment-46654042 • Water Education Foundation on water use: www.WaterEducation.org/post/food-facts-how-much-water-does-it-take-produce ⚠️ We have no affiliation with any plant-milk producer. If you don\'t need milk for your cereal/coffee, then consider switching to what every other adult mammal on this planet drinks: Water. ⚠️ Work on this photo began in July 2021, before the Tahoe megafire or LA fires. Our hearts go out to all those impacted 💚🌲🐾 Ultimately, we\'re all in this together 🌏 and the finger pointing here is merely to point out an important point that many are missing. Unfortunately, truth doesn\'t go away. HIGHLY Recommended: 🔥 www.GotEvil.org 😱 www.DairyIsScary.org 🏋️\u200d♀️ Cowspiracy (on Netflix + Amazon) 🌞 California girls: www.WhyPlantMilk.info/#lt-moviesholder-sh 🌱 Game Changers (on Netflix + Amazon) 🌏 www.Hope-TheProject.com (Free) 💚 What the Health (Netflix + Amazon) 🐾 www.WatchDominion.com (Free) 🙏 Thank you for looking, and for signing the petition: www.GotMilked.org', 'Defending the Integrity of Real Food: Why Clear Labels Matter in the Fight for Dairy and Beef At AGPROfessionals, we support the hardworking families who drive America’s beef and dairy industries. Their products nourish the nation with tradition and unmatched nutrition. Unfortunately, some multinational food corporations promote plant-based imitations disguised as milk and meat. Despite clear regulatory definitions, plant-based alternatives made from nuts, seeds, and grains, such as almond milk, oat milk, or vegan “cheese,” continue to exploit dairy terms on their packaging. This causes consumer confusion and dilutes the market integrity of real dairy products. That’s why we back renewed bipartisan efforts at the federal level to restore honesty and transparency in food labeling. The DAIRY PRIDE Act of 2025 is one such law that protects the exclusive use of dairy terminology for products made from real milk. Truth in Labeling: The DAIRY PRIDE Act Reintroduced in July 2025, the Defending Against Imitations and Replacements of Yogurt, Milk, and Cheese To Promote Regular Intake of Dairy Everyday (DAIRY PRIDE) Act seeks to enforce long-standing FDA standards of identity for dairy products. The DAIRY PRIDE Act is sponsored by Wisconsin Senator Tammy Baldwin, Idaho Senator James Risch, Maine Senator Susan Collins, Vermont Senator Peter Welch, Minnesota Senator Amy Klobuchar, Maine Senator Angus King, Pennsylvania Senator John Fetterman, New York Senator Kirstin Gillibrand, Idaho Senator Mike Crapo, South Dakota Senator Mike Rounds, Nebraska Senator Pete Ricketts, Kansas Senator Roger Marshall, and Minnesota Senator Tina Smith. The DAIRY PRIDE Act mandates the FDA: • Enforce existing labeling laws for milk, cheese, and yogurt. • Reserve dairy terms strictly for products from hooved mammals. • Issue clear enforcement guidance within 90 days. • Report back to Congress within two years on its progress. This bipartisan bill is supported by a coalition of dairy state senators and industry groups, including the National Milk Producers Federation, Dairy Farmers of America (DFA), and the American Farm Bureau Federation (AFBF). Fake Meat & Dairy: Misleading Claims, Inferior Nutrition At the heart of this debate is nutritional honesty. The marketing behind oat milk, almond milk, or meat-like substitutes such as Impossible™ and Beyond™ products implies nutritional equivalency, or even superiority, over animal-sourced foods. That couldn’t be further from the truth. Even organizations like the American Academy of Pediatrics warn against using plant-based “milks” as a substitute for dairy in growing children due to the lack of essential nutrients. Real Dairy and Beef Offer: • Complete proteins with all essential amino acids • Naturally occurring vitamin B12, crucial for neurological health • High levels of calcium, potassium, and vitamin D • Bioavailable iron and zinc in beef, key for growth and immune function Plant-Based Imitations: • Often require artificial fortification to match real dairy’s nutrient profile • May contain additives, gums, and oils and other products to simulate the taste and texture of real dairy and meat products • Lack essential nutrients found naturally in dairy and meat • Since they are an imitation product, they are more processed than the wholesome foods they imitate The Bigger Threat: Corporate Agendas to End Animal Agriculture The battle over labeling is not merely semantic, it is ideological. Major food-tech companies and so-called “advocacy” backed startups have openly declared their intention to “end animal agriculture.” These companies are not allies of agriculture. They are actively working to dismantle the very livelihoods that feed rural America. Their goal? Replace real meat and milk with synthetic, lab-grown, or plant-derived substitutes. Despite overwhelming evidence to the contrary, their aggressive lobbying efforts aim to reframe animal agriculture as outdated, unethical, or environmentally harmful. What’s needed now is legislative backbone, consumer education, and unwavering industry advocacy to combat this agenda. Defend Farmers, Defend the Label At AGPROfessionals, we understand what’s at stake and have been advocating for honest labeling of dairy products for a number of years. Clear labeling is not just about protecting terminology, and keeping ideological companies from hijacking words, it is about defending truth, transparency, and the hardworking families behind real food. Dairy Farmers of America (DFA) had this to say about the DAIRY PRIDE Act, “Words matter. Nutrition matters. Truth in labeling matters. For years, plant-based products have used dairy terms, despite lacking the same nutrients, science, and trust that come with real milk. These imitators continue to confuse consumers and capitalize on the decades of investment dairy farmers have made in building a safe, nutritious, and sustainable food system, all without playing by the same rules. The DAIRY PRIDE Act would require the U.S. Food and Drug Administration to enforce its own definition of dairy. Products that don’t come from a cow, or another lactating mammal, shouldn’t be labeled milk, cheese, or yogurt. We’re committed to supporting efforts that protect the integrity of real dairy and the farmers behind it.” At AGPROfessionals, we urge Congress to pass the DAIRY PRIDE Act. We would also like to see them expand similar protections for beef and other animal proteins. We support policies that protect consumer trust, rural economies, and the values of American agriculture. Let’s call “milk” what it is, a product of the cow, not a concoction of emulsified nuts. Contact your elected officials and urge them to support the DAIRY PRIDE Act of 2025. Stand with the farmers and ranchers who produce the safest, most nutritious food supply in the world. Because when we protect the label, we protect the people behind it. For referenes and to read the bill language visit this article on our website. At AGPROfessionals, we understand what’s at stake and have been advocating for honest labeling of dairy products for a number of years. Clear labeling is not just about protecting terminology and keeping ideological companies from hijacking words; it is about defending truth, transparency, and the hardworking families behind real food.', 'A study by Science Daily shows that not only will eating PASTA up to three [3] times a week NOT make you fat, but it might even help you become slimmer... Many of us eat macaroni salad with hard-boiled eggs, and eat eggs & bacon (the meaning of Carbonara) often with flour-made pancakes-especially during the summer. So, why not substitute flour-made pasta and instead enjoy Spaghetti alla Carbonara? The four [4] classic Roman Pasta Dishes can be prepared in minutes once you gather the basic ingredients — and they are easy to prepare stove top by first pre-cooking the various pasta super al dente for simply 6-minutes while the Guanciale cooks in a skillet like American thick-cut bacon... The classic Roman pasta dish, Spaghetti alla Carbonara, uses Guanciale (pig cheeks), eggs, fresh-cracked pepper, and Pecorino Romano Cheese. We were ecstatic to find imported Guanciale available at @[100063777873572:2048:Gabriel\'s Supermarket], in Rotterdam (Schenectady), New York. I’ve seen a piece of packaged Guanciale in the small specialty cooler at Capri Imports Italian Deli and Catering before. However, I think you have to buy the whole piece since it’s not unwrapped and in the deli counter case with other meats and cheeses that are sliced and sold by the number of slices and/or pound. I’ll ask Anthony Sementelli if he opens the package and cuts Guanciale to the customer’s desired amount the next time I shop at Capri. In Rome, Amatriciana, Carbonara, Gricia, and Cacio Pepe are the four classic pasta dishes Italians love... In our @[100063660996459:2048:Discover Schenectady] region, @[100054197093481:2048:Pede Brothers] sells a variety of dry and fresh-frozen pasta—as well as @[100042367524056:2048:Capri Imports Italian Deli and Catering], @[471165999724908:274:Gabriel\'s Supermarket], @[100094620512003:2048:Lagioia Deli], and @[100093123658034:2048:Sahr\'s] Cash & Carry—open to the public... @[100063717456098:2048:For the love of Bacon] also sell a domestic version of Guanciale that is produced from their farm-raised pigs... And, I\'m sure that many of the Italian delis listed above can order Guanciale imported from ITALY upon your special request. WAS CARBONARA CREATED BY BRITISH AND US TROOPS? An authentic creamy Carbonara e Spaghetti contains no cream. Some food historians say that the BLACK PEPPER in Spaghetti alla Carbonara is meant to impart the traditional flavor to the dish and the long history described by many food historians of the meaning of the word, CARBONARI, which means "CHARCOAL [COAL] BURNERS"... The Rome-Berlin Axis Coalition was formed in 1936 between Italy and Nazi Germany... In 1940, Italy ONLY produced 4.4 megatonnes (Mt) of COAL—by comparison, Great Britain produced 224.3 Mt of coal—and Germany produced 364.8 Mt of coal. The Berlin-Rome-Axis Treaty was the result of increasing dependence on German COAL following the League of Nations (precursor to the UNITED NATIONS) sanctions... The aftermath of the Berlin-Rome-Axis Treaty saw the increasing ties between Fascist Benito Mussolini, Italy, and Nazi Germany. Popular lore points to WWII American GIs and British soldiers as the inspiration behind the creation of the pasta dish Carbonara, during Rome’s liberation by Allied forces... Fascist Italy entered World War II (WWII) as an Axis Power in 1940 and was defeated in North Africa and the Soviet Union. US and Allied landings in Sicily, Italy led to the fall of the Fascist regime and the new government surrendered to the Allies in September 1943... On October 13, 1943, the government of Italy declared war and turned on its former Axis partner Nazi Germany and joined the battle on the side of the Allies. By the end of WWII, Italy was occupied by the Allied forces of Britain and the United States, and Fascist Benito Mussolini was murdered on April 28, 1945... Adolf Hitler, chancellor and dictator of Nazi Germany, and the rise of the Third Reich from 1933 — with the signing of the REICH CONCORDAT between the Vatican — committed suicide via a gunshot to the head on April 30, 1945... In 1946, Italian voters abolished the monarchy for its cooperation with Fascist Mussolini and Nazi Germany—this marked the end of the Kingdom of Italy. BRITISH AND US SOLDIERS\' BACON AND EGGS CREATED CARBONARA? According to legend, these soldiers asked local chefs in Italy to use their bacon rations and powdered eggs to make a dish, resulting in carbonara’s first written reference in the newspaper La Stampa in the 1950s. Regardless of what story you believe about the creation of Spaghetti alla Carbonara, the authentic recipe is part of Italy\'s four [4] main pasta dishes. Some chefs mix the whisked egg yolks, whole eggs, and pepper in a bowl —- coat the spaghetti in the melted Guanciale fat in a skillet —- and then stir the pasta and cheese together with a little of the warm pasta water. This method does not always result in a smooth Carbonara sauce… Enjoy these recipes and as an alternative try tempering your Carbonara sauce in a bowl with a drizzle of the warm melted Guanciale fat and starchy pasta water —- that you bloomed freshly ground black peppercorns in beforehand —- then whisk in the grated Pecorino Romano cheese. Also, you can use a blender to make Carbonara sauce by drizzling the same fresh pepper-seasoned melted fat through the chute of your blender —- then add the grated cheese and run the blender with drips of the starchy pasta water until you get a silky smooth sauce. Dive into Traditional Roman Pasta Recipes and the secrets of these Italian delights... The SLOW FOOD INTERNATIONAL movement originated in ROME, ITALY in 1989 as a response to the growth of fast food and other changes in the food system worldwide... Slow Food began with the founding of its forerunner organization, Arcigola, in 1986 to resist the opening of a McDonald\'s near the Spanish Steps in Rome. In 1989, the founding manifesto of the international Slow Food movement was signed in Paris, France, by delegates from 15 countries... After 35 years the SLOW FOOD MOVEMENT now boasts millions of members from more than 1,500 local chapters in more than 150 countries—focusing on fair and SUSTAINABLE food available to All. #amatriciana, Pasta alla #gricia, #carbonara, and #cacioepepe #foodandtravel #tasteofschenectadyandbeyond #pasta VISIT our website https://TASTEandSHOP.com']
