Representative_Docs: ['𝗧𝗵𝗶𝘀 𝗶𝘀 𝗮𝗹𝗮𝗿𝗺𝗶𝗻𝗴: The last 10 years have been the hottest years on record. We are now barreling toward an irreversible climate tipping point — threatening the air we breathe, the water we drink and nature’s most iconic and beloved wildlife. The United States pulled out of the Paris Climate Accords, but it’s not too late to work together and avoid the worst impacts of climate change. We all need to do our part: That’s why we’re counting on 2,391 more conservation supporters to join us before 11:59 p.m. tonight, and we noticed you haven’t signed yet. 𝗣𝗹𝗲𝗮𝘀𝗲, 𝘄𝗶𝗹𝗹 𝘆𝗼𝘂 𝗮𝗱𝗱 𝘆𝗼𝘂𝗿 𝗻𝗮𝗺𝗲 𝗻𝗼𝘄 𝘁𝗼 𝗷𝗼𝗶𝗻 𝘂𝘀 𝗮𝗻𝗱 𝗱𝗲𝗺𝗮𝗻𝗱 𝗮𝗰𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻 𝘁𝗼 𝗽𝗿𝗼𝘁𝗲𝗰𝘁 𝗻𝗮𝘁𝘂𝗿𝗲 𝗮𝗻𝗱 𝗮𝘃𝗼𝗶𝗱 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝘄𝗼𝗿𝘀𝘁 𝗶𝗺𝗽𝗮𝗰𝘁𝘀 𝗼𝗳 𝗰𝗹𝗶𝗺𝗮𝘁𝗲 𝗰𝗵𝗮𝗻𝗴𝗲?,𝐔𝐑𝐆𝐄𝐍𝐓 𝐟𝐫𝐨𝐦 𝐑𝐞𝐮𝐭𝐞𝐫𝐬: “Massive fires fueled by climate change led global forest loss to smash records in 2024.” Rampant deforestation is driving the Amazon to an ecological tipping point. Last year, the worst drought ever recorded in the Amazon led to record-breaking forest destruction due to wildfires. And as more trees are cleared, the forest’s capacity to retain moisture drastically decreases, setting the stage for even more severe fire seasons. It’s a dangerous domino effect—and it’s unfolding right now. We’re working with partners to protect the Amazon rainforest, but we need your help. This wildfire season, we set a goal for 5,000 supporters to stand with us before 11:59 p.m. tonight, but we’re still 482 signers short — and it looks like your name is missing. 𝐒𝐨 𝐩𝐥𝐞𝐚𝐬𝐞, 𝐚𝐝𝐝 𝐲𝐨𝐮𝐫 𝐧𝐚𝐦𝐞 𝐧𝐨𝐰 𝐭𝐨 𝐩𝐫𝐨𝐭𝐞𝐜𝐭 𝐭𝐡𝐞 𝐀𝐦𝐚𝐳𝐨𝐧 𝐫𝐚𝐢𝐧𝐟𝐨𝐫𝐞𝐬𝐭.', '𝗧𝗵𝗶𝘀 𝗶𝘀 𝗮𝗹𝗮𝗿𝗺𝗶𝗻𝗴: The last 10 years have been the hottest years on record. We are now barreling toward an irreversible climate tipping point — threatening the air we breathe, the water we drink and nature’s most iconic and beloved wildlife. The United States pulled out of the Paris Climate Accords, but it’s not too late to work together and avoid the worst impacts of climate change. We all need to do our part: That’s why we’re counting on 2,391 more conservation supporters to join us before 11:59 p.m. tonight, and we noticed you haven’t signed yet. 𝗣𝗹𝗲𝗮𝘀𝗲, 𝘄𝗶𝗹𝗹 𝘆𝗼𝘂 𝗮𝗱𝗱 𝘆𝗼𝘂𝗿 𝗻𝗮𝗺𝗲 𝗻𝗼𝘄 𝘁𝗼 𝗷𝗼𝗶𝗻 𝘂𝘀 𝗮𝗻𝗱 𝗱𝗲𝗺𝗮𝗻𝗱 𝗮𝗰𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻 𝘁𝗼 𝗽𝗿𝗼𝘁𝗲𝗰𝘁 𝗻𝗮𝘁𝘂𝗿𝗲 𝗮𝗻𝗱 𝗮𝘃𝗼𝗶𝗱 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝘄𝗼𝗿𝘀𝘁 𝗶𝗺𝗽𝗮𝗰𝘁𝘀 𝗼𝗳 𝗰𝗹𝗶𝗺𝗮𝘁𝗲 𝗰𝗵𝗮𝗻𝗴𝗲?,🦦Rampant deforestation in the Amazon is bringing some of Earth’s most magnificent wildlife to the brink of extinction — including giant otters, jaguars and the hyacinth macaw. 🔥Severe wildfires are exacerbating the deforestation crisis and destroying the habitats of critically endangered species. 🌎This vital tropical rainforest is essential to sustaining life on Earth, which is why we’re working with governments and communities to protect the Amazon from deforestation and prevent further damage — but we need your help. 𝐖𝐞’𝐫𝐞 𝐥𝐨𝐨𝐤𝐢𝐧𝐠 𝐟𝐨𝐫 𝟒𝟖𝟐 𝐦𝐨𝐫𝐞 𝐬𝐢𝐠𝐧𝐞𝐫𝐬 𝐭𝐨 𝐬𝐭𝐚𝐧𝐝 𝐰𝐢𝐭𝐡 𝐮𝐬 𝐛𝐞𝐟𝐨𝐫𝐞 𝐦𝐢𝐝𝐧𝐢𝐠𝐡𝐭 𝐭𝐨𝐧𝐢𝐠𝐡𝐭 𝐭𝐨 𝐬𝐡𝐨𝐰 𝐭𝐡𝐞𝐢𝐫 𝐬𝐮𝐩𝐩𝐨𝐫𝐭 𝐟𝐨𝐫 𝐩𝐫𝐨𝐭𝐞𝐜𝐭𝐢𝐧𝐠 𝐭𝐡𝐞 𝐀𝐦𝐚𝐳𝐨𝐧 𝐫𝐚𝐢𝐧𝐟𝐨𝐫𝐞𝐬𝐭, 𝐛𝐮𝐭 𝐢𝐭 𝐥𝐨𝐨𝐤𝐬 𝐥𝐢𝐤𝐞 𝐲𝐨𝐮𝐫 𝐧𝐚𝐦𝐞 𝐢𝐬 𝐬𝐭𝐢𝐥𝐥 𝐦𝐢𝐬𝐬𝐢𝐧𝐠. 𝐏𝐥𝐞𝐚𝐬𝐞, 𝐰𝐢𝐥𝐥 𝐲𝐨𝐮 𝐚𝐝𝐝 𝐲𝐨𝐮𝐫 𝐧𝐚𝐦𝐞 𝐧𝐨𝐰 𝐭𝐨 𝐩𝐫𝐨𝐭𝐞𝐜𝐭 𝐭𝐡𝐞 𝐀𝐦𝐚𝐳𝐨𝐧 𝐫𝐚𝐢𝐧𝐟𝐨𝐫𝐞𝐬𝐭 𝐭𝐡𝐢𝐬 𝐰𝐢𝐥𝐝𝐟𝐢𝐫𝐞 𝐬𝐞𝐚𝐬𝐨𝐧?', "We Need To Protect Petaluma's Ecosystem My “day job” is being a landscape photographer. I go out in nature and try to capture its beauty in a single image. I love my job, but as a person whose profession involves “being observant”, I regularly notice the damage that we humans inflict on the environment, and to other species who have been here long before us. We gratuitously make these other species’ lives difficult, if not impossible, and we often do it just for our own convenience. Native Americans lived here for thousands of years. A Coast Miwok elder told an anthropologist in 1932 that “deer and elk used to be plentiful in the valley this side of Petaluma — just like cattle there [and that] Nicasio people got acorns from the Petaluma Valley.” Ducks and geese flew up from the Petaluma River and its tributaries so thick as to obliterate the sun for an hour at a time, and seasonal swarms of monarch butterflies passing through the Petaluma Valley a mile wide, several miles long, forced the Lekatuit there to take refuge for sometimes a full day. Of course, we don’t have that ecosystem anymore because we have destroyed over 90% of the wetlands and paved over prime habitat around Petaluma, blocking animals from their traditional feeding grounds and migration paths. We rarely notice the destruction because it happens over years. Friends tell me how they used to see foxes and badgers on their property years ago, now they are seen no more. You would think we have done enough damage, but apparently the City of Petaluma doesn’t think so, because it continues to do damage on a regular basis, approving construction near riverbanks and creeks, and in wetlands. There is a lot of talk about decarbonization and environmental justice, but no one seems to be talking about the ecosystem that we all live in. We have precious few open spaces left within city limits, but we can still protect these remaining places if we act decisively. Bad Decisions Mike Healy approved 180 luxury homes at Sid Commons on February 24th, 2020, which is partially located in the wetlands of the Corona Reach, the city-council overruled the unanimous vote of our planning commissioners who voted against re-zoning this parcel to allow the construction of these units. I and several other Petaluma citizens banded together and sued to stop construction. There has been no construction on that parcel to the present date, and the Friends of the Petaluma River are now trying to acquire parcels in that area as part of a Petaluma River Greenway project. Even though we lost the case, it has been a significant victory to delay that project this long. We may just save this piece of critical habitat after all. Another long-term ecological battle was over Scott Ranch, the Davidon proposal to build luxury homes at our urban/wildland interface. As part of this project, there is unfortunately an expansion of Helen Putnam Park that is another human-centric development with two parking lots, paved trails, and pedestrian bridges. It has little regard for the existing wildlife at this location, and some Petalumans call it a “Disneyland Park”. This is habitat for the threatened red-legged frog, and it seems that this development will help make it go extinct in Petaluma. Both Mike Healy and Brian Barnacle approved this project even though it violates many of our environmental standards, including the VMT (vehicle miles traveled) standard. All the traffic to the park and new homes will be using D Street, which is already overburdened. This is bad planning all around. The Biggest Upcoming Environmental Battle The biggest upcoming battle will be over the Corona Reach wetlands and floodway. There is currently a proposal to build over 600 homes with over 1100 parking spaces there (this proposal is by the same property owner that owns Sid Commons). If that ever happens, it will be an ecological disaster and will possibly negate the flood protection provided by the weir and flood walls constructed in the upper Petaluma River by the Army Corps of Engineers at a cost of over 40 million dollars. The flood walls border the edge of the Payran neighborhood. One of the options in our upcoming general plan is to put 3-story homes in the Corona Reach as well as a new shopping center (including stores like a Costco). This is where the “Rainier Connector” has been proposed over the years to alleviate traffic. A Rainier Connector would not alleviate traffic, what it would do is open our largest remaining wetlands and meadows to rampant development, and it would further congest traffic. If these proposals ever happen, this critical habitat will be lost forever. Another option in the upcoming General Plan is to turn the Corona Reach into Open Space. I think the entire remainder of the Corona Reach should be turned into a low-impact park that is restored as much as possible to its natural state. This will reduce flooding and provide critical habitat for the few remaining native plants and animals that are still in city limits. It can also be a nature path for hikers and bikers to cross town without having to deal with vehicle traffic on busy streets like Washington Street or other crosstown connectors. Other Important Parcels to Protect In District 6 itself, we have the Paula Lane Nature Preserve, a piece of land that local citizens have been helping to restore for years. The city seems to be working at cross-purposes with these volunteers, instead of cooperating with them. Casa Grande High School students have been working for years to restore a fishery on the east side of town through their nonprofit “The United Anglers of Casa Grande”. I would support taking an inventory of all ecologically important parcels within the city, and work with any groups that are trying to protect them, and I would like to get all these stakeholders together to work on a comprehensive plan to protect and enhance all these parcels. I have been an advocate for the environment ever since I could remember. As soon as I was no longer a starving student, I joined the Sierra Club (I just got a certificate of appreciation from them celebrating 35 years of membership). Locally, I am on the board of “Protect Wild Petaluma”, a nonprofit dedicated to protecting our remaining wild places in Petaluma. I am on the steering committee of 350 Petaluma, part of 350.org, a nonprofit that is dedicated to reducing the carbon in our atmosphere. My wife Moira is a co-founder of ReLeaf Petaluma, and I created their first website so that they could obtain their first grant. My wife advocated for the planting of NATIVE trees by ReLeaf, and that organization is doing a great job of increasing the tree canopy in Petaluma. I have also done extensive work for the Sonoma Land Trust, photographing their properties to help with fundraising and leading free photography workshops on their properties to engage the public in land conservation. I could go on, but let’s just say that I take environmental protection very seriously. Because of my background, several Petaluma environmental advocates in town support my candidacy for city council, including former Council Member David Keller, who has advocated for the protection of the Corona Reach for literally decades. Conclusion We have to take the environmental crisis seriously and not just put up banners saying that we are “Climate-Ready” when our actions say otherwise. I would like to apply my knowledge and skills to protecting Petaluma’s ecosystem. I not only want to stop environmental destruction in our city, but I also want to “rewild” parts of Petaluma and help us become a “regenerative city”. This would greatly improve the quality of life for all living beings in Petaluma, including us humans. ------------------------- A Recent Endorsement “District 6 residents would do well to consider voting for Lance Kuehne. His platform of managed growth, green spaces and community involvement should resonate with anyone looking for a balanced approach. Lance is for preserving Petaluma’s architectural heritage while grappling with the need to add affordable housing stock; managing growth while preserving green spaces and protecting the river, and for a more collaborative, transparent government. Read Lance’s blog, “The Kuehne Observer”—I’ll bet it will resonate with you!” Lydia Asselin – Retired Petaluma Architect ------------------------- To help spread the message of this campaign, consider making a donation. I am running against two incumbents, and changing how our city is run isn’t easy. To donate, click this link https://share.hsforms.com/1Qnm1ZuPYRLOsY5sDTFgTOQrtxgh"]

Name: 33_pennsylvania_pa_lightning plan_shapiro
Representative_Docs: ['David N. Taylor, PMA’s President & CEO, testified against House Bill 2277, Governor Shapiro’s Pennsylvania Reliable Energy Sustainability Standard (PRESS) plan, emphasizing its potential negative impact on the manufacturing sector, which heavily relies on affordable and reliable energy. Taylor highlighted that Pennsylvania’s current energy market has successfully reduced electricity rates and greenhouse gas emissions since the 1996 Electricity Generation Customer Choice and Competition Act. House Bill 2277 mandates over half of Pennsylvania’s electricity to come from specified sources, including 35% from "Tier I" sources like wind and solar. Taylor argued that this shift would harm existing energy sources, such as natural gas and coal, risking premature plant closures and threatening grid reliability while placing significant financial burdens on consumers. He also questioned the feasibility of new technologies like small modular nuclear reactors and fusion reactors, which are not yet viable. Governor Shapiro’s PRESS plan also raises concerns about the environmental impact and land use required for solar and wind power, the challenges of energy storage, and the reliance on foreign sources for battery components. He pointed out the deplorable human rights issues associated with renewable energy supply chains.', 'In York, Voith Hydro isn’t just a company. It’s been the backbone of employment in the community for generations. But recently, many jobs were at risk. That changed when federal clean energy funding found its way to Voith. With federal Clean Energy Tax Credits, Voith will save nearly $6 Million and reinvest in new machinery — keeping family-sustaining jobs in York. 🙌 Watch Voith’s story to meet Carl, Amy, and Jason — three of the hundreds of Voith employees whose jobs are now better-protected thanks to federal funding.,In York, Voith Hydro isn’t just a company. Representative_Docs: ['The Arctic Refuge is our nation’s last remaining intact wilderness—and it has never been touched by Big Oil. But Project 2025 makes it clear that anti-environmental lawmakers in Congress and Trump’s cabinet want to drill in the Arctic—even at the expense of caribou herds, polar bears, millions of birds, and the culture of the Gwich’in people. Your donation today helps ensure we can protect the Arctic from Trump and his allies. The Sierra Club has already helped win protection for 13 millions acres of the Arctic, but we need permanent protections so that the Arctic is off-limits to Big Oil for good. Trump was the first president to remove protections from the Arctic Refuge in 2017, making it clear that his priority for his second presidency is lining the pockets of his fossil fuel CEO friends and greedy allies in Congress. Your donation helps our legal and political advocacy teams gear up to protect even more of the Arctic from Big Oil. Representative_Docs: ['Clean energy is booming across Illinois. It’s helping American families, small businesses and nature. New research shows that in Illinois, from now to 2032, clean energy tax credits will: ☀️ Support more than 10,500 jobs annually ☀️ Support nearly $1.4 billion in annual economic value added ☀️ Provide a return of $2.24 for each federal dollar invested ☀️ Generate over $692 million in household income annually ☀️ Yield more than $169.3 million in local, state and federal tax revenue annually ☀️ Improve clean air, drinking water and wildlife habitat Despite all the good these tax credits are doing for Illinoisans; Congress is considering repealing these credits, which will hurt our economy and our environment. That’s why we need Congress to maintain these credits. https://www.nature.org/en-us/about-us/who-we-are/how-we-work/policy/clean-energy-tax-credits/?en_txn1=s_fbo.reg_mw.x.x', 'The strain on America’s power grid is growing — from AI, crypto, and increased demand. Energy tax credits are a smart solution. They’re helping build new U.S.-based energy projects, lower utility costs, and reduce our reliance on foreign supply chains. They also support hardworking families and retirees who can’t afford to see their electricity bills go up again. Congress should strengthen what’s working — not cut it.,The strain on America’s power grid is growing — from AI, crypto, and increased demand. Energy tax credits are a smart solution. They’re helping build new U.S.-based energy projects, lower utility costs, and reduce our reliance on foreign supply chains. They also support hardworking families and retirees who can’t afford to see their electricity bills go up again. Congress should strengthen what’s working — not cut it.,The strain on America’s power grid is growing — from AI, crypto, and increased demand. Energy tax credits are a smart solution. They’re helping build new U.S.-based energy projects, lower utility costs, and reduce our reliance on foreign supply chains. They also support hardworking families and retirees who can’t afford to see their electricity bills go up again. Congress should strengthen what’s working — not cut it.,The strain on America’s power grid is growing — from AI, crypto, and increased demand. Energy tax credits are a smart solution. They’re helping build new U.S.-based energy projects, lower utility costs, and reduce our reliance on foreign supply chains. They also support hardworking families and retirees who can’t afford to see their electricity bills go up again. Congress should strengthen what’s working — not cut it.', 'In a time of 🚀skyrocketing energy demand and 🔖 rising prices, the Inflation Reduction Act is doing exactly what it was designed to do: lower costs, accelerate new energy supply, and protect American consumers. Repealing it now won’t just stall progress. It will drive electricity prices higher across the country. ⚡️U.S. electricity demand is projected to rise by as much as 90 GW in the next five years—largely driven by data centers and AI infrastructure. The Brattle Group estimates total demand could grow 20–25% by 2035. And when supply can’t keep up with demand, costs go up. That’s basic economics. The IRA’s clean energy 45Y and 48E tax credits are two of the best tools we have to directly boost supply while keeping costs down. They’ve already helped get 19 GW of clean energy projects shovel ready. However, the current federal plan to meet our rising energy needs is to scrap clean energy sources—by way of stripping these tax credits—and promote major growth in natural gas and Small Modular (nuclear) Reactors (SMRs). The evidence showing the short-sightedness of this policy is rapidly growing. 📈 Study after study—from CEBA, Brattle, Energy Innovation, and NERA—show that repealing clean energy tax credits would raise electricity prices by an average of $200 per household each year. At a time when American families are already feeling the squeeze from rising costs, we can’t afford to stall progress. Offshore wind is reliable, American-made, and ready to scale today. And, because of the IRA and its tax credits, it’s poised to help meet soaring demand while keeping prices down. Repealing the IRA won’t “balance the budget.” It will raise electricity bills, slow new energy development, and send utility bills climbing. The path forward isn’t retreat. It’s momentum. More projects. More power. More savings. ✅ Share this post. ✅Call your local representative and senator. Tell them why America needs offshore wind. #OSWatWork #OffshoreWind #SupplyChain #Policy #RenewableEnergy']

Name: 36_farmtopower_supportfarmers_fightfakenews_ruralrevitalization
Representative_Docs: ['In New Mexico, farmers and ranchers have always known how to work with the land, not against it. 🌾☀️💨 From wind to solar, local farmers and ranchers are helping power a sustainable future. They deserve the freedom to decide how their land is used, and if wind or solar helps them stay on that land, that should be celebrated. Sign our petition if you support farmers and ranchers hosting renewables: https://farmtopower.org/new-mexico/ #FarmToPower #SupportLocalFarmers #FarmLife #HeartlandFarming #SustainableFarming #ResilientFuture #LocalAg #AgAdvocacy #NextGenFarmers #AgInnovation #FarmersSupportingFarmers #SustainableAgriculture #AgCommunity #FutureOfFarming #AgSustainability #RuralRevitalization #FightFakeNews #SupportFarmers #FarmersRights #EnergyMix', 'More and more farmers are deciding to host wind or solar energy on their land to protect the future of their farm without abandoning the farming lifestyle they love.🌾 Think farmers don’t like renewables? Think again! 👇 #FarmToPower #SupportLocalFarmers #FarmLife #HeartlandFarming #SustainableFarming #ResilientFuture #LocalAg #AgAdvocacy #NextGenFarmers #AgInnovation #FarmersSupportingFarmers #SustainableAgriculture #AgCommunity #FutureOfFarming #AgSustainability #RuralRevitalization #FightFakeNews #SupportFarmers #FarmersRights #EnergyMix', 'Our team had the chance to chat with New Mexico rancher Jerry Elkins about agrivoltaics. Not only does Jerry already have solar on his land, but he\'s also planning to add more solar or wind energy! Check out his story below, and DM us if you\'d like to be featured for #FarmerFriday! 💡☀️🐑 "I live in New Mexico, where my family and I farm hay to support our horses and cattle. We\'ve taken steps to incorporate renewable energy into our operations by installing solar panels, and we\'re currently researching the potential of adding wind turbines to our setup. I believe that using a combination of solar, wind, and other renewable energy sources offers a more comprehensive approach to sustainability. It\'s not just about meeting our energy needs but also about creating a resilient and eco-friendly farm. One area that has caught my interest in agrivoltaics- combining agriculture with solar power to enhance both crop production and energy generation. I\'m excited to explore how this could benefit our farm and others in the community. Renewable energy isn\'t just a trend; it\'s a necessary step forward for the future of farming and sustainable land management." -Jerry Elkins, New Mexico. #FarmToPower #SupportLocalFarmers #FarmLife #HeartlandFarming #SustainableFarming #ResilientFuture #LocalAg #AgAdvocacy #NextGenFarmers #AgInnovation #FarmersSupportingFarmers #SustainableAgriculture #AgCommunity #FutureOfFarming #AgSustainability #RuralRevitalization #FightFakeNews #SupportFarmers #FarmersRights #EnergyMix']

Name: 38_texas_819_sb 819_texas energy
Representative_Docs: ["Texas Critical Data Centers, a joint venture between Sharon AI and New Era Helium, has announced its intention to acquire 200 acres in Ector County, strategically positioning itself to develop a 250-megawatt AI-ready data center within the Permian Basin. This ambitious project, valued at over $8 billion, aims to leverage the region’s abundant natural gas, land, skilled workforce, and energy generation capabilities, potentially positioning the Permian Basin as a leading data center hub in North America. The development is expected to provide notable economic benefits for Ector County, including reducing the county's courthouse debt ahead of schedule and lowering overall tax rates, thereby fostering sustainable long-term growth. Moreover, the region is seen as a competitive hotspot, with other major data center and hyperscaler projects valued at up to $50 billion in capital investments considering Ector County for future expansion. This initiative underscores a broader trend of data infrastructure growth driven by regional energy resources and technological demand, promising significant economic and strategic advantages for Ector County and the broader Texas region. @[100074189066894:2048:Ector County Judge Dustin Fawcett]t, aims to implement substantial improvements to the community while reducing taxes by shifting much of the tax burden onto this emerging power generation sector. If brought to fruition these companies would also pay sales tax , adding as much as $1.5 million dollars per month to the county's sales tax revenue.", "The Texas Senate lead the way on using 5 Billion Dollars to help Electrical generation using natural gas, as the Federal Government has tremendous subsidies for Wind and Solar energy producers. Since we must have reliable power in Texas, we're now looking at how to keep up with forecasts that say we need 150 MW of power daily in 2030, which is a 76% increase. While the @[100077835098866:2048:Joe Biden] Administration continues to prioritize solar and wind energy subsidies, Texas has prioritized natural gas, as we must have base load energy. We passed SB 2627, by Chairman @[100046952173768:2048:Dr. Charles Schwertner], and I proudly co-authored the bill, to establish a level playing field between the federal subsidies and Texas by establishing the Texas Energy Fund. The Legislature earmarked $5 billion to start the fund in 2024-25 and Texas voters approved the creation of the fund last November. To great collective disappointment, and I quite frankly was aghast at the @[100069322625189:2048:Public Utility Commission of Texas] handling of this issue this past Thursday in a Senate Finance Hearing, we learned they nearly allowed a potentially fraudulent low-interest loan application that Aegle Power submitted on behalf of their company & @[100064391755549:2048:NextEra Energy Resources] to slip through their vetting process! NextEra Energy claimed they were not even aware that the application was submitted on their behalf! Read about it now in the @[100064765418303:2048:The Dallas Morning News] Phil Jankowski’s article: https://www.dallasnews.com/news/politics/2024/09/05/texas-energy-fund-scrutinized-after-loan-applicant-was-rejected-for-supplying-false-info/ (https://www.dallasnews.com/news/politics/2024/09/05/texas-energy-fund-scrutinized-after-loan-applicant-was-rejected-for-supplying-false-info/) How Aegle’s application slipped by @[100052469103430:2048:Deloitte], who is being paid $107 million by the PUC to vet these applications, is unbelievable and never to be repeated! My point is the PUC is no longer just a rate-setting organization. They need to be better stewards of energy funds and taxpayers' dollars in Texas, and be on the lookout for FRAUD! -Senator Paul Bettencourt", "Why rural Texans are fighting a proposed natural gas-fired power plant For a decade, Julie Drake and her husband Kevin have enjoyed life on their property in the gentle hills and pastures of Lee County, only about an hour outside of Austin but feeling like a world away from the big city. However, earlier this year, they learned of a company’s plans to build a massive gas-fired power plant within a mile of their property. Sandow Lakes Energy’s proposed plant is one of scores of new natural gas-fired power plants planned across the U.S., with the surge driven by cheap gas from hydraulic fracturing and horizontal drilling and compounded by increased demand from artificial intelligence and cryptocurrency computer centers. The air pollution, health risks, and potentially depressed real estate values from the construction of a power plant are not what Drake and her neighbors bargained or are willing to accept. “We moved out here for the quiet, slower pace, clean air – just a better way of life,” Drake said. In Texas alone there are currently over 150 proposed projects to build new or expand existing gas-fired power plants, according to Environmental Integrity Project research based on data from state and federal agencies. The increased burning of fossil fuels – instead of using clean energy, like solar or wind – to satisfy this growing hunger for electricity threatens U.S. climate goals. The Sandow plant could emit nearly 4 million tons per year of greenhouse gases, as much as about 847,000 cars and trucks driven for a year, according to information in the company's application for an air permit. The plant could also emit up to 250 tons per year of nitrogen oxides and nearly 89 tons per year of volatile organic compounds, both of which are smog-forming pollutants that contribute to asthma attacks and long-term lung damage. The facility could also emit up to nearly 152 tons per year of fine particle pollution, which can trigger heart attacks, along with more than 165 tons per year of toxic carbon monoxide. All this has made Julie Drake and her neighbors want to fight the plant’s proposed location. Read the full story: https://news.oilandgaswatch.org/post/in-deep-red-texas-neighbors-fight-gas-power-plant-next-door---one-of-scores-proposed-across-u-s"]

Representative_Docs: ['🏡 ¡Residentes de Everett SOLAMENTE! — LETI Presenta la próxima clase de "Nuestro Aire-Nuestra Salud" (NANS)🌟 ¿Cuándo? 🗓️ Sábados, 19 y 26 de Abril ⏰ 10:00 a.m. - 1:00 p.m. ¿Dónde? 📍 Everett, WA 98204 (Se requiere registrarse para obtener la ubicación completa) \u200bRequisitos: ✅ ¡Ser residente de Everett! ✅ Participación solo \u200bserá \u200ben persona y en español. ✅ \u200bSolo se dará un filtro de aire por hogar. ✅Regístrese aquí: https://forms.gle/16JpUrByJuzo53E39 \u200b✨🎁¡Y eso no es todo! Al asistir a las dos clases, ¡cada participante recibirá un filtro de aire valorado en aproximadamente $200! ¡Únase a una increíble iniciativa para mejorar la calidad del aire y promover la salud en nuestra comunidad! Este programa está diseñado para educar y empoderar a los miembros de la comunidad Latina sobre cómo la contaminación del aire afecta la salud familiar. ¡Además, queremos fomentar tu participación en los temas de calidad del aire a nivel local! 🌱💚 Para más información o si tienes preguntas, ¡no dudes en contactar a Marisol Bejarano! 📧Marisol@letiwa.org/📱(425) 510-8792 ¡Esperamos verte pronto y juntos mejorar el aire que respiramos! ✨ @followers', 'Desde Nevada, Omar nos muestra que la energía limpia no es un lujo: es parte de la vida diaria de miles de familias trabajadoras ☀️💡 El sol puede reducir tus gastos, mejorar tu salud y darte independencia energética. Pero todo eso está en riesgo si se recortan las inversiones en energía limpia. ✊ Envíale un mensaje a tu representante y defiende un futuro solar para todos. 👉 No te toma ni un minuto, ¡solo haz clic y envía! 📲 [Link en nuestra Bio] eco.sachamama.org ------------ From Nevada, Omar shows us that clean energy isn’t a luxury it’s a daily reality for thousands of working families ☀️💡 The sun can lower your bills, boost your health, and give you energy independence. But all of that is at risk if clean energy investments are cut. ✊ Send a message to your representative and defend a solar future for all. 👉 Takes less than a minute, just click and send! 📲 [Link in our bio] clean.sachamama.org #EnergíaSolar #AhorroEnergético #TransiciónJusta #ComunidadesLatinas #EnergíaLimpia #SolarEnergy #EnergySavings #FairTransition #LatinoCommunities #CleanEnergy #Sachamama', '¡Residentes de Florida que pagan más de $79 por la electricidad deben tener en cuenta esto! El nuevo programa solar, a nivel estatal, ayuda a los dueños de casas a aprovechar todos los incentivos y reembolsos solares federales, estatales y locales. Con el programa de recompra de energía de Florida - ¡Nunca ha habido un mejor momento para cambiar la energía solar que el 2024! El Programa de recompra de energía permite a los dueños de casa de Florida vender su energía no utilizada a las compañías de electricidad locales para ayudar a reducir los costos de energía en todo el estado. Con las compañías tradicionales de electricidad aumentando sus tarifas un promedio del 6% al año, este programa de recompra de energía permite a los clientes de servicios públicos eliminar su factura de energía mientras también se les paga por el exceso de energía que producen a través de la energía solar. ¿Por qué harían esto las empresas de servicios públicos? Porque pueden recomprar la energía más barata de lo que podrían producirla ellos mismos, por lo tanto, pueden estabilizar sus propias tarifas en toda la ciudad al pagarle por cambiar a energía solar. ¿Cuál es el truco? Solo 1 de cada 4 hogares califica para este programa. Haga clic en [Más información] para averiguar si su casa califica. Name: 43_students_teaching_teaching climate_climate education
Representative_Docs: ['Teaching about climate has become an important topic for both formal and non-formal educators and EEAI is here to help you navigate that space! We have two opportunities this October for educators to learn more about teaching climate in their classrooms, nature centers, and other spaces. First, the Teaching Climate Summit at Severson Dells Nature Center in Rockford, IL is an in-person two day event designed to prepare 5th-12th grade educators to tackle climate with youth. Learn more and register here: https://eeaoi.wildapricot.org/event-5724164 Second, for the first time EEAI is offering a virtual Teaching Climate eeCourse! This 4-week online, asynchronous, course is designed to provide Grades 4-12 formal and non-formal educators with an introduction to environmental education and the skills necessary to integrate climate change environmental education into their classrooms, homeschool classrooms, and programs. Learn more and register here: https://eeaoi.wildapricot.org/event-5871615 And best of all, ISBE credit hours are available for both programs!', 'BRING CLIMATE INTO YOUR CLASSROOM WHILE HITTING THE STANDARDS! ✅ FREE PD ✅ FREE Lunch! ✅ FREE BOOKS! 📅 Wednesday, June 25th, 2025 🕣 8:30 AM – 3:30 PM 📍 RSVP here: https://tinyurl.com/mszcv32x ~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~ Event Details: ⏰ Welcome: 9:30 – 9:50 AM ⏰ Free Lunch!: 12:00 – 12:45 PM ⏰ Personal Prep: 2:45 – 3:30 PM ⏰ Wrap-up with a family-style game of Climate Jeopardy! ~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~ 🕵️\u200d♂️ Who are The Energy Detectives? We’ll begin with a read-along of books from our free series, The Energy Detectives, followed by a lesson from our free, standards-aligned curriculum! We’ll share our favorite resources to boost your climate literacy AND your confidence in teaching the subject ~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~ 🌱 What’s a Climate Café? A climate café offers an open space to reflect on our thoughts, feelings, and experiences about climate change—without diving straight into solutions or opinions. This space allows us to slow down, connect, and become more present and available for these experiences in ourselves and in our students. 📧 Contact: info@coolercommunities.org 📚 Follow us: @theenergydetectives 🌐 Supported by: Berkshire Feigenbaum Foundation https://www.feigenbaumfoundation.org/ Berkshire Innovation Center https://www.berkshireinnovationcenter.com/home', 'For MASS STEM Week - High school teachers in Massachusetts are invited FREE Climate Education Teacher Training at Salem State University on Saturday, October 19, 2024. Principals and local leaders are encouraged to nominate their teachers who will receive a free a book, curriculum, and teacher training with 5 PD (Professional Development) credits. Join the wave of educators preparing their students to be ready for careers in sustainability. Climate Education can help your students become competitive in a variety of fields and plays a crucial role in how we shape the future generation! “I am pleased to help present this incredibly important curriculum and training to local teachers. The sooner young people learn about the climate change crisis, the sooner they will hopefully start to make the necessary changes,” -Keith Ratner, Chair of Salem State’s Geography and Sustainability Department. For more information or any questions send an email to SalemStateUPD@stonesoupleadership.org Learn more: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=MC2NvqyW9V0 Visit: https://stonesoupleadership.org/storage/2024/09/ssu-climate-ed-teacher-training-pr.pdf']

Name: 44_na team_edpr na_edpr_na
Representative_Docs: ['🎮🐈 From coding a Farm Simulator video game to collaborating with local animal shelters to operate a small animal fostering program, FFA-ers are creative thinkers and hard workers when it comes to leveraging agriculture to support their communities. @[100067480800245:2048:National FFA Organization] Supervised Agricultural Experiences (SAEs) give young innovators in farming the opportunity to bring their ideas to life and gain foundational skills they’ll utilize throughout their careers. EDPR NA is proud to provide grants across the U.S. to support students in these efforts, including financing one of the grants awarded this year here in Texas.', "We 💚 Randolph County’s ag community, and we're invested in its continued flourishing. For this year’s @[100063695615085:2048:Purdue Extension Randolph County] Fair, our EDPR NA team was pleased to recognize the diligent work of young 4-Hers and FFA members by participating in this year’s livestock auction with an evenly distributed donation across exhibitors. The future of the fair is brighter than ever: we have also proudly contributed a $100,000 donation to @[100057871170752:2048:Randolph County, IN Fairground Capital Improvement Projects] toward updates to the grounds that will include major renovations to the Best Way Center and the construction of a new Show Arena facility. The initiative also received a $1.65 million donation from the Randolph County government, financed by economic development agreement payments paid to the county by our Headwaters Wind Farm and Riverstart Solar Park. The improvement team broke ground on the renovations last month, and the new facilities are expected to be completed in time for the 2025 fair! If you’d like to support or learn more, visit randolphcountyfairgrounds.com.", '💛💙 Year after year, our EDPR NA team is beyond impressed with the sharpness, drive, and leadership of the FFA-ers we meet at the annual convention and in the communities where we work. It was a banner year at the 97th annual @[100067480800245:2048:National FFA Organization] Convention in Indianapolis. Members of our EDPR NA team attended, as did more than 65,000 FFA-ers. It was a busy few days for our team: we staffed a booth with our friends from the @[100057687751661:2048:KidWind Project] and highlighted some of the exciting intersections between the skills students garner through their FFA work and career trajectories in the renewable energy sector. EDPR NA Executive Vice President Tom LoTurco participated as a guest speaker at the Environmental and Natural Resources Career Development Awards Event and other team members served as judges for various events. Thank you to the FFA members who stopped by our booth to say hi, and congratulations to all those who showed projects. See you next year! #FFA24']

This aid included military assistance, economic development programs, and humanitarian relief. The success of this intervention in stabilizing these countries helped solidify the use of foreign aid as a key component of U.S. Cold War strategy. 𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝗖𝗼𝗹𝗱 𝗪𝗮𝗿 𝗘𝗿𝗮: 𝗔𝗶𝗱 𝗮𝘀 𝗮 𝗦𝘁𝗿𝗮𝘁𝗲𝗴𝗶𝗰 𝗜𝗻𝘀𝘁𝗿𝘂𝗺𝗲𝗻𝘁 𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝗙𝗼𝘂𝗻𝗱𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝗼𝗳 𝗨𝗦𝗔𝗜𝗗 (𝟭𝟵𝟲𝟭) 𝗮𝗻𝗱 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝗔𝗹𝗹𝗶𝗮𝗻𝗰𝗲 𝗳𝗼𝗿 𝗣𝗿𝗼𝗴𝗿𝗲𝘀𝘀 The creation of the 𝗨.𝗦. 𝗔𝗴𝗲𝗻𝗰𝘆 𝗳𝗼𝗿 𝗜𝗻𝘁𝗲𝗿𝗻𝗮𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻𝗮𝗹 𝗗𝗲𝘃𝗲𝗹𝗼𝗽𝗺𝗲𝗻𝘁 (USAID) in 1961 marked the institutionalization of U.S. foreign aid. President 𝗝𝗼𝗵𝗻 𝗙. 𝗞𝗲𝗻𝗻𝗲𝗱𝘆 established USAID to coordinate and administer U.S. development assistance. Around the same time, Kennedy launched the Alliance for Progress, a ten-year plan to promote economic development and social reforms in Latin America. • 𝘼𝙡𝙡𝙞𝙖𝙣𝙘𝙚 𝙛𝙤𝙧 𝙋𝙧𝙤𝙜𝙧𝙚𝙨𝙨: The Alliance for Progress aimed to reduce poverty and inequality in Latin America, thereby reducing the appeal of communism. However, the program had mixed results, with some countries experiencing significant growth while others achieved little progress. Food for Peace (PL 480) and the Green Revolution The Food for Peace Program (Public Law 480), launched in 1954, aimed to use U.S. agricultural surpluses to provide food aid to developing countries. This program laid the groundwork for the Green Revolution, a series of agricultural innovations that dramatically increased food production in Latin America and Asia. • 𝙂𝙧𝙚𝙚𝙣 𝙍𝙚𝙫𝙤𝙡𝙪𝙩𝙞𝙤𝙣: The Green Revolution, which began in the 1960s, involved the introduction of high-yield crop varieties, modern irrigation techniques, and chemical fertilizers. While it succeeded in increasing food production, it also had significant environmental and social consequences, such as increased inequality among farmers. 𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝗥𝗼𝗹𝗲 𝗼𝗳 𝗡𝗼𝗻-𝗚𝗼𝘃𝗲𝗿𝗻𝗺𝗲𝗻𝘁𝗮𝗹 𝗢𝗿𝗴𝗮𝗻𝗶𝘇𝗮𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻𝘀 (𝗡𝗚𝗢𝘀) 𝗗𝘂𝗿𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝗖𝗼𝗹𝗱 𝗪𝗮𝗿 During the Cold War, 𝗡𝗼𝗻-𝗚𝗼𝘃𝗲𝗿𝗻𝗺𝗲𝗻𝘁𝗮𝗹 𝗢𝗿𝗴𝗮𝗻𝗶𝘇𝗮𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻𝘀 (NGOs) played an increasingly important role in U.S. humanitarian and development aid. Organizations like 𝗖𝗔𝗥𝗘, 𝗢𝘅𝗳𝗮𝗺, and 𝗖𝗮𝘁𝗵𝗼𝗹𝗶𝗰 𝗥𝗲𝗹𝗶𝗲𝗳 𝗦𝗲𝗿𝘃𝗶𝗰𝗲𝘀 partnered with the U.S. government to deliver aid in conflict zones and developing countries. • 𝘾𝘼𝙍𝙀 𝙋𝙖𝙘𝙠𝙖𝙜𝙚𝙨: One of the most iconic examples of NGO-led humanitarian aid was the CARE Package, which provided food and supplies to people in postwar Europe and later in developing countries. CARE became a key partner of USAID, helping to deliver food aid, medical supplies, and educational materials. 𝘾𝙖𝙨𝙚 𝙎𝙩𝙪𝙙𝙮: U.S. Aid in Vietnam and Southeast Asia The U.S. intervention in Vietnam (1955-1975) highlighted the complexities of using aid as a tool of military strategy. In addition to military aid, the U.S. provided billions of dollars in economic development assistance to South Vietnam in an attempt to create a stable, anti-communist state. • 𝙎𝙩𝙧𝙖𝙩𝙚𝙜𝙞𝙘 𝙃𝙖𝙢𝙡𝙚𝙩 𝙋𝙧𝙤𝙜𝙧𝙖𝙢: One of the most controversial aid programs was the Strategic Hamlet Program, which aimed to relocate rural populations into fortified villages to prevent them from being influenced by the Viet Cong. However, the program was largely unsuccessful and contributed to growing resentment against the U.S. and the South Vietnamese government. •𝙋𝙤𝙨𝙩-𝙒𝙖𝙧 𝙍𝙚𝙘𝙤𝙣𝙨𝙩𝙧𝙪𝙘𝙩𝙞𝙤𝙣 After the war, the U.S. also provided aid to neighboring countries like Cambodia and Laos, which had been heavily impacted by the conflict. This aid included both humanitarian relief for refugees and long-term development assistance. ꘏ ꘏ ꘏ ꘏ ꘏ ꘏ ꘏ 𝗣𝗼𝘀𝘁-𝗖𝗼𝗹𝗱 𝗪𝗮𝗿: 𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝗦𝗵𝗶𝗳𝘁 𝗳𝗿𝗼𝗺 𝗖𝗼𝗻𝘁𝗮𝗶𝗻𝗺𝗲𝗻𝘁 𝘁𝗼 𝗗𝗲𝘃𝗲𝗹𝗼𝗽𝗺𝗲𝗻𝘁 𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝗪𝗮𝘀𝗵𝗶𝗻𝗴𝘁𝗼𝗻 𝗖𝗼𝗻𝘀𝗲𝗻𝘀𝘂𝘀 𝗮𝗻𝗱 𝗦𝘁𝗿𝘂𝗰𝘁𝘂𝗿𝗮𝗹 𝗔𝗱𝗷𝘂𝘀𝘁𝗺𝗲𝗻𝘁 𝗣𝗿𝗼𝗴𝗿𝗮𝗺𝘀 (𝗦𝗔𝗣𝘀) In the late 1980s and 1990s, U.S. development policy was heavily influenced by the 𝗪𝗮𝘀𝗵𝗶𝗻𝗴𝘁𝗼𝗻 𝗖𝗼𝗻𝘀𝗲𝗻𝘀𝘂𝘀, a set of economic reforms that promoted 𝗳𝗿𝗲𝗲 𝗺𝗮𝗿𝗸𝗲𝘁𝘀, 𝗽𝗿𝗶𝘃𝗮𝘁𝗶𝘇𝗮𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻, and 𝗱𝗲𝗿𝗲𝗴𝘂𝗹𝗮𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻. These ideas were enforced through 𝗦𝘁𝗿𝘂𝗰𝘁𝘂𝗿𝗮𝗹 𝗔𝗱𝗷𝘂𝘀𝘁𝗺𝗲𝗻𝘁 𝗣𝗿𝗼𝗴𝗿𝗮𝗺𝘀 (SAPs), often administered by the World Bank and IMF. • 𝗦𝗔𝗣𝘀: Structural Adjustment Programs required developing countries to implement austerity measures, such as cutting government spending and privatizing state-owned enterprises, in exchange for financial assistance. While these programs were intended to stabilize economies, they were widely criticized for exacerbating poverty and inequality. 𝗛𝘂𝗺𝗮𝗻𝗶𝘁𝗮𝗿𝗶𝗮𝗻 𝗜𝗻𝘁𝗲𝗿𝘃𝗲𝗻𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻𝘀 𝗶𝗻 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝟭𝟵𝟵𝟬𝘀: 𝗦𝗼𝗺𝗮𝗹𝗶𝗮, 𝗛𝗮𝗶𝘁𝗶, 𝗮𝗻𝗱 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝗕𝗮𝗹𝗸𝗮𝗻𝘀 The end of the Cold War brought a new focus on humanitarian interventions in conflict zones. U.S. military and aid interventions in Somalia (1992), Haiti (1994), and the Balkans (1995) were framed as efforts to protect human rights and promote peace, though they often had mixed results. 𝙎𝙤𝙢𝙖𝙡𝙞𝙖 (1992-1993): The U.S. intervention in Somalia, known as Operation Restore Hope, aimed to provide humanitarian relief to civilians suffering from famine and civil war. However, the mission quickly became entangled in local conflicts, culminating in the Battle of Mogadishu in 1993, which led to the withdrawal of U.S. forces. 𝙃𝙖𝙞𝙩𝙞 (1994): In 1994, U.S. forces intervened in Haiti to restore the democratically-elected government of Jean-Bertrand Aristide, who had been overthrown in a military coup. While the intervention successfully reinstated Aristide, Haiti continued to face significant political and economic challenges. 𝘽𝙖𝙡𝙠𝙖𝙣𝙨 (1995): U.S. involvement in the Balkans, particularly in Bosnia and Kosovo, was motivated by the desire to prevent ethnic cleansing and promote regional stability. The U.S. played a key role in brokering the Dayton Accords, which ended the Bosnian War in 1995. 𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝗥𝗶𝘀𝗲 𝗼𝗳 𝗚𝗹𝗼𝗯𝗮𝗹 𝗛𝗲𝗮𝗹𝘁𝗵: 𝗛𝗜𝗩/𝗔𝗜𝗗𝗦 𝗮𝗻𝗱 𝗣𝗘𝗣𝗙𝗔𝗥 In the late 1990s and early 2000s, global health became a major focus of U.S. foreign aid. The President\'s Emergency Plan for AIDS Relief (PEPFAR), launched by President George W. Bush in 2003, committed billions of dollars to combat the HIV/AIDS epidemic, particularly in Sub-Saharan Africa. • 𝙋𝙀𝙋𝙁𝘼𝙍: PEPFAR is widely regarded as one of the most successful U.S. foreign aid programs, helping to provide antiretroviral treatment to millions of people living with HIV/AIDS. The program also made significant investments in health infrastructure and prevention efforts, such as distributing condoms and promoting safe sex practices. 𝘾𝙖𝙨𝙚 𝙎𝙩𝙪𝙙𝙮: U.S. Intervention in Somalia (1992-1993) The U.S. intervention in Somalia, also known as Operation Restore Hope, was one of the most significant humanitarian missions of the post-Cold era. Initially aimed at providing food aid to civilians affected by famine and civil war, the mission quickly became entangled in local power struggles. • 𝘽𝙖𝙩𝙩𝙡𝙚 𝙤𝙛 𝙈𝙤𝙜𝙖𝙙𝙞𝙨𝙝𝙪: The mission culminated in the Battle of Mogadishu in October 1993, when U.S. forces clashed with Somali militias loyal to warlord 𝗠𝗼𝗵𝗮𝗺𝗲𝗱 𝗙𝗮𝗿𝗿𝗮𝗵 𝗔𝗶𝗱𝗶𝗱. The downing of two U.S. Black Hawk helicopters and the deaths of 18 American soldiers during this battle became the focal point of U.S. involvement in Somalia. The incident, popularly known as "Black Hawk Down," led to a public outcry in the U.S., and ultimately, the withdrawal of American troops from Somalia. • 𝘾𝙤𝙣𝙨𝙚𝙦𝙪𝙚𝙣𝙘𝙚𝙨: The failure of the mission in Somalia had lasting impacts on U.S. foreign policy. It led to a more cautious approach toward humanitarian interventions, often referred to as the "Somalia Syndrome." This reluctance to intervene in complex humanitarian crises is believed to have influenced U.S. inaction during the Rwandan Genocide in 1994, where an estimated 800,000 people were killed. ꘏ ꘏ ꘏ ꘏ ꘏ ꘏ ꘏ 𝟮𝟭𝘀𝘁 𝗖𝗲𝗻𝘁𝘂𝗿𝘆: 𝗪𝗮𝗿 𝗼𝗻 𝗧𝗲𝗿𝗿𝗼𝗿 𝗮𝗻𝗱 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝗠𝗶𝗹𝗶𝘁𝗮𝗿𝗶𝘇𝗮𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻 𝗼𝗳 𝗔𝗶𝗱 𝗣𝗼𝘀𝘁-𝟵/𝟭𝟭 𝗙𝗼𝗿𝗲𝗶𝗴𝗻 𝗣𝗼𝗹𝗶𝗰𝘆 𝗮𝗻𝗱 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝗥𝗼𝗹𝗲 𝗼𝗳 𝗔𝗶𝗱 𝗶𝗻 𝗔𝗳𝗴𝗵𝗮𝗻𝗶𝘀𝘁𝗮𝗻 𝗮𝗻𝗱 𝗜𝗿𝗮𝗾 The terrorist attacks of 𝗦𝗲𝗽𝘁𝗲𝗺𝗯𝗲𝗿 𝟭𝟭, 𝟮𝟬𝟬𝟭, fundamentally shifted U.S. foreign policy, particularly in relation to humanitarian aid and development. In the aftermath of the attacks, the U.S. launched the 𝗚𝗹𝗼𝗯𝗮𝗹 𝗪𝗮𝗿 𝗼𝗻 𝗧𝗲𝗿𝗿𝗼𝗿, leading to military interventions in Afghanistan (2001) and Iraq (2003). Aid became increasingly intertwined with military objectives, particularly in efforts to stabilize war-torn regions and "win hearts and minds." • 𝘼𝙛𝙜𝙝𝙖𝙣𝙞𝙨𝙩𝙖𝙣 (2001-𝙋𝙧𝙚𝙨𝙚𝙣𝙩): After the U.S.-led invasion in 2001, Afghanistan became a major recipient of U.S. foreign aid. USAID and other U.S. agencies were tasked with rebuilding the country\'s infrastructure, promoting democratic governance, and providing humanitarian relief to civilians affected by the war. However, much of this aid was delivered in conjunction with military operations, blurring the lines between humanitarianism and military strategy. • 𝙋𝙧𝙤𝙫𝙞𝙣𝙘𝙞𝙖𝙡 𝙍𝙚𝙘𝙤𝙣𝙨𝙩𝙧𝙪𝙘𝙩𝙞𝙤𝙣 𝙏𝙚𝙖𝙢𝙨 (𝙋𝙍𝙏𝙨): One of the kev moshanisms for delivering aid in Afghal Stop the use of Provincial Reconstruction Teams (PRTS), which combined military personnel with civilian experts to carry out development projects. While PRTs helped to rebuild schools, clinics, and roads, they were often criticized for making aid workers targets of insurgent attacks because of their close association with military forces. • 𝗜𝗿𝗮𝗾 (𝟮𝟬𝟬𝟯-𝟮𝟬𝟭𝟭): Similarly, after the U.S. invasion of Iraq in 2003, billions of dollars in aid were allocated for the reconstruction of Iraq\'s infrastructure and the establishment of democratic institutions. However, corruption, mismanagement, and insecurity hampered these efforts. The 𝗨.𝗦. 𝗔𝗴𝗲𝗻𝗰𝘆 𝗳𝗼𝗿 𝗜𝗻𝘁𝗲𝗿𝗻𝗮𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻𝗮𝗹 𝗗𝗲𝘃𝗲𝗹𝗼𝗽𝗺𝗲𝗻𝘁 (USAID) and other aid organizations focused on rebuilding critical infrastructure, such as water treatment plants and electrical grids, but progress was slow, and many projects were left incomplete due to ongoing violence. 𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝗛𝘂𝗺𝗮𝗻𝗶𝘁𝗮𝗿𝗶𝗮𝗻-𝗗𝗲𝘃𝗲𝗹𝗼𝗽𝗺𝗲𝗻𝘁 𝗡𝗲𝘅𝘂𝘀: 𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝗖𝗵𝗮𝗹𝗹𝗲𝗻𝗴𝗲 𝗼𝗳 𝗙𝗿𝗮𝗴𝗶𝗹𝗲 𝗦𝘁𝗮𝘁𝗲𝘀 In the 21st century, U.S. aid policy increasingly embraced the concept of the 𝗵𝘂𝗺𝗮𝗻𝗶𝘁𝗮𝗿𝗶𝗮𝗻-𝗱𝗲𝘃𝗲𝗹𝗼𝗽𝗺𝗲𝗻𝘁 𝗻𝗲𝘅𝘂𝘀, recognizing that many of today\'s crises are protracted, requiring both immediate humanitarian relief and long-term development solutions. The focus on fragile states, where governance is weak and conflict is ongoing, became a central theme in U.S. aid policy. • 𝗙𝗿𝗮𝗴𝗶𝗹𝗲 𝗦𝘁𝗮𝘁𝗲𝘀: Countries like Sudan, South Sudan, Yemen, and Syria have been key targets of U.S. humanitarian and development aid. These countries face challenges such as weak state institutions, ongoing conflict, and poor infrastructure. U.S. aid has aimed to address these challenges by promoting governance reforms, supporting peacebuilding initiatives, and providing humanitarian assistance to displaced populations. • 𝗦𝗲𝗰𝘂𝗿𝗶𝘁𝘆 𝗮𝗻𝗱 𝗗𝗲𝘃𝗲𝗹𝗼𝗽𝗺𝗲𝗻𝘁: The U.S. increasingly viewed development aid as essential for maintaining global security, with aid programs designed to prevent the collapse of fragile states and reduce the risk of terrorism. However, critics have argued that this approach has led to the 𝗺𝗶𝗹𝗶𝘁𝗮𝗿𝗶𝘇𝗮𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻 𝗼𝗳 𝗮𝗶𝗱, where development goals are subordinated to security objectives, potentially compromising the neutrality of humanitarian efforts. 𝗨.𝗦. 𝗖𝗹𝗶𝗺𝗮𝘁𝗲 𝗖𝗵𝗮𝗻𝗴𝗲 𝗔𝗱𝗮𝗽𝘁𝗮𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻 𝗶𝗻 𝗚𝗹𝗼𝗯𝗮𝗹 𝗔𝗶𝗱 As awareness of 𝗰𝗹𝗶𝗺𝗮𝘁𝗲 𝗰𝗵𝗮𝗻𝗴𝗲 has grown, U.S. foreign aid has begun to address the challenges posed by environmental degradation, extreme weather events, and rising sea levels. Climate change disproportionately affects the world\'s poorest countries, leading to food insecurity, displacement, and conflict over scarce resources. • 𝙁𝙚𝙚𝙙 𝙩𝙝𝙚 𝙁𝙪𝙩𝙪𝙧𝙚 𝙄𝙣𝙞𝙩𝙞𝙖𝙩𝙞𝙫𝙚: Launched in 2010, the Feed the Future initiative is the U.S. government\'s global hunger and food security program. It aims to increase agricultural productivity, reduce poverty, and promote resilience to climate change. The initiative focuses on regions most vulnerable to food insecurity, such as Sub-Saharan Africa and South Asia. •𝗖𝗹𝗶𝗺𝗮𝘁𝗲 𝗔𝗱𝗮𝗽𝘁𝗮𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻: The U.S. has also funded efforts to help developing countries adapt to the impacts of climate change. This includes investments in renewable energy, water management, and disaster preparedness. However, the U.S. has faced criticism for not contributing enough to global climate finance, particularly in comparison to its historical contributions to greenhouse gas emissions. 𝗖𝗮𝘀𝗲 𝗦𝘁𝘂𝗱𝘆: 𝗨.𝗦. 𝗔𝗶𝗱 𝗶𝗻 𝗔𝗳𝗴𝗵𝗮𝗻𝗶𝘀𝘁𝗮𝗻 (𝟮𝟬𝟬𝟭-𝗣𝗿𝗲𝘀𝗲𝗻𝘁) The U.S. mission in Afghanistan, following the 2001 invasion, represents one of the largest and longest U.S. foreign aid efforts in history. Over two decades, the U.S. spent over $145 billion on reconstruction and development in Afghanistan. However, the results have been mixed, with significant challenges in governance, corruption, and security undermining development efforts. • 𝙀𝙙𝙪𝙘𝙖𝙩𝙞𝙤𝙣: One of the notable successes of U.S. aid in Afghanistan was the expansion of access to education, particularly for girls. By 2018, the number of children attending school had increased from around 1 million in 2001 to over 9 million, with nearly 40% of them being girls. •𝘾𝙤𝙧𝙧𝙪𝙥𝙩𝙞𝙤𝙣 𝙖𝙣𝙙 𝙈𝙞𝙨𝙢𝙖𝙣𝙖𝙜𝙚𝙢𝙚𝙣𝙩: However, efforts to rebuild Afghanistan were plagued by widespread corruption and mismanagement. Representative_Docs: ['#highcitedpaper #LiquidHydrogen #Maritime #transportation 🧐Titel: An Extensive Review of Liquid Hydrogen in Transportation with Focus on the Maritime Sector @[43262856368:69:Norwegian University of Science and Technology (NTNU)] @University of Trieste 🎞Abstract： The European Green Deal aims to transform the EU into a modern, resource-efficient, and competitive economy. The REPowerEU plan launched in May 2022 as part of the Green Deal reveals the willingness of several countries to become energy independent and tackle the climate crisis. Therefore, the decarbonization of different sectors such as maritime shipping is crucial and may be achieved through sustainable energy. Hydrogen is potentially clean and renewable and might be chosen as fuel to power ships and boats. Hydrogen technologies (e.g., fuel cells for propulsion) have already been implemented on board ships in the last 20 years, mainly during demonstration projects. Pressurized tanks filled with gaseous hydrogen were installed on most of these vessels. However, this type of storage would require enormous volumes for large long-range ships with high energy demands. One of the best options is to store this fuel in the cryogenic liquid phase. This paper initially introduces the hydrogen color codes and the carbon footprints of the different production techniques to effectively estimate the environmental impact when employing hydrogen technologies in any application. Afterward, a review of the implementation of liquid hydrogen (LH2) in the transportation sector including aerospace and aviation industries, automotive, and railways is provided. Then, the focus is placed on the maritime sector. The aim is to highlight the challenges for the adoption of LH2 technologies on board ships. Different aspects were investigated in this study, from LH2 bunkering, onboard utilization, regulations, codes and standards, and safety. Finally, this study offers a broad overview of the bottlenecks that might hamper the adoption of LH2 technologies in the maritime sector and discusses potential solutions. ⛱Keywords: liquid hydrogen; hydrogen color; carbon footprint; transport; maritime sector; review; cost; safety 👀more information: https://bit.ly/3XTmTK8', 'Will the government let energy companies claim tax breaks for fossil fuel-based hydrogen? When hydrogen is burned, it releases only water vapor and no greenhouse gases, making it a potentially climate-friendly fuel for uses like steel production and cement manufacturing depending on how the hydrogen is made. But hydrogen is expensive to produce, which is why the Biden Administration and Congress provided up to $100 billion in taxpayer subsidies for hydrogen production in the 2022 Inflation Reduction Act. Most hydrogen used today is produced using natural gas, which has a large greenhouse gas footprint. But hydrogen can also be produced cleanly using solar or wind power and water. Under draft rules the Internal Revenue Service (IRS) released in December, the cleaner a hydrogen source is, the more tax breaks a producer can receive. Those proposed rules have ignited a debate over how strict those rules should be. On one side are those who think the hydrogen industry, still in its early stages, needs flexibility to grow quickly and bring down the cost of hydrogen enough to substitute it for fossil fuels. On the other side are those who say the industry needs strict guardrails to ensure a transition to hydrogen actually reduces greenhouse gas emissions instead of incentivizing fossil fuel-based hydrogen. Meanwhile, some energy companies want to see subsidies go to hydrogen produced from natural gas. Billions of dollars and dozens of massive construction projects are at stake. As of September 2024, there were 65 proposed new hydrogen manufacturing plants and 12 expansions in the planning stages across the U.S., and two already under construction in Baytown, Texas and in Genessee County, in upstate New York, according to public records on file in the Oil & Gas Watch database. If built, the proposed projects could emit more than 1.3 million tons of carbon dioxide per year, about as much as 295,000 passenger vehicles driving for a year. Read the full story here: https://news.oilandgaswatch.org/post/a-debate-over-100-billion-in-taxpayer-subsidies-could-set-the-fate-of-the-u-s-hydrogen-industry', 'Lithium battery EVs step aside? He who masters hydrogen energy rules the world! Did you know the U.S. is building its second Hydrogen Valley? Hydrogen energy is hailed as the “ultimate clean energy” and is indispensable for achieving net-zero emissions by 2050. According to research by Fuji Keizai, the global hydrogen fuel cell market size is expected to reach $52 billion by 2027, with an estimated annual growth rate of 31.4% from 2020 to 2027. This indicates that hydrogen energy and fuel cells will be critical investment opportunities, and stocks of related companies will see explosive growth! According to the International Energy Agency’s 2025 Net Zero Carbon Emissions Report, achieving net-zero carbon emissions by 2050 will require global hydrogen demand to reach 530 million tons. Currently, 17 countries have proposed comprehensive strategies for hydrogen energy development. The U.S. 2050 Net Zero Carbon Emissions Roadmap has allocated 9%-12% of energy supply to hydrogen, signaling its readiness to take off. It’s the next big trend in the U.S. stock market! Elon Musk has declared: He who masters hydrogen energy rules the world, and Tesla will transition from lithium-ion batteries to hydrogen fuel cells by 2025! It’s estimated that by 2030, a budget of nearly $900 billion will be invested, potentially driving trillion-dollar business opportunities across related industries! Name: 47_students_school_education_schools
Representative_Docs: ['🚨 Exciting Announcement 🚨 I’m thrilled to officially announce my candidacy for the Tomah Area School Board! As a proud member of this community since 2014, a dedicated public servant, and a parent, I am committed to working tirelessly to ensure our schools remain a place of excellence for every student, educator, and family. Why I’m Running: Our schools are at a crossroads, and it’s time to take bold steps to strengthen the foundation of our education system. Transparency in school board operations is my top priority. We need to rebuild trust with the community by ensuring everyone has access to the decisions that shape our children’s futures. By modernizing our technology and making our actions more accessible, we can create a school board that is a true beacon of transparency and accountability. I also believe that success begins in the classroom—not the boardroom. Our educators are the heart of our schools, and they deserve the support, respect, and resources necessary to help our students thrive. My goal is to empower them and ensure our board always operates with a mindset of service to students, educators, and the entire community. About Me: I moved to Tomah in 2014 after serving five years in the Marine Corps and transitioning to civilian service for the Wisconsin National Guard. My wife and I also spent a year teaching abroad, where I gained a deeper understanding of the power of education to transform lives. Today, I am continuing my 14-year career in public service by promoting the use of artificial intelligence within the U.S. Government to make better decisions for the citizens of our country. My wife, an educator in the Tomah Area School District, and I are raising our two amazing daughters here—one attending Tomah Middle School and the other enrolled in RVA. Living in Tomah is a choice I’ve made and will continue to make because I believe in the strength and values of our community. Looking Ahead: If elected, I will work tirelessly to ensure every decision we make is transparent, thoughtful, and focused on the well-being of our students and families. Together, we can create sustainable solutions to fund and improve our schools without overburdening our community. Let’s work together to build trust, empower our educators, and provide the opportunities our children need to succeed. I humbly ask for your support as we embark on this journey to make a positive difference for the future of Tomah’s schools. 💬 Let’s Connect: I’d love to hear your thoughts, concerns, and ideas. Comment below or send me a message to share your vision for our schools! Thank you for your support! Let’s make this happen together. 💪📚', 'Thank you, Lia! 🐾❤🐾❤ "My name is Lia Wagner and I am a freshman at BGHS. I am writing to you today to share how crucial the upcoming Bowling Green City Schools operational levy is to our community. I participate in various extracurriculars and non-core classes at BGHS that may no longer be available if the upcoming levy fails. My current activities include: The Bobcat Marching Band, BGHS Concert Band, BGHS Treble Choir, Drama Club, the All–School Musical, Art Club, Spanish Club and StuCrew. My high school experience and life has been so much better because of these experiences. These groups have given me opportunities to get involved in my school and participate in activities for the community, make new friends, meet new people, carry on family traditions and gain new experiences and life skills. "Our performing groups are \'home\' for so many students. Marching band has proven extremely helpful for me in the transition to high school, as we had the opportunity to connect with other band members before the school year even started through band camp and rehearsals. We bring energy and school spirit with our music during pre-game performances and halftime at football games. We get to march in parades for our community and represent our school. We make amazing memories, and I am so happy when I am with the band! Our band and choir represent Bowling Green, not only during our concerts, but at events like Solo & Ensemble and Contest, where we had three Bowling Green groups compete at the state level this spring! Being a part of the Choir has also been an amazing experience. You can really see and feel the group’s connection both in class and while performing on stage. Everybody is always working hard and supporting each other so that we can sound our best. Symphonic Band and Madrigals also show our school’s immense gratitude and honor the veterans in our community at our Veteran’s Day Assembly. "Our amazing clubs do so much. StuCrew runs and helps with many school and community events such as Homecoming, Powderbuff and Powderpuff games, spirit weeks and more at school, plus numerous community activities including the Pancake Breakfast, and is dedicated to making both a better place. Drama Club spreads the love for theater throughout our community and school. We work hard to put on many performances throughout the school year and represent BGHS. During our time in our shows we learn about working with others, as well as other lessons that we will benefit from as we become adults. Clubs like Spanish Club and French Club teach us about other cultures. Art Club enriches students with a passion for art and spreads color throughout the school and community. In the younger grades, we have PACE classes that teach students social skills, job application writing, responsibilities and research skills that we will use well into college and the workforce. "Now imagine all of these activities being gone. What would happen to our students’ mental health and what would the streets of our community look like? Would students care about school? Would they stay away from negative behaviors like drugs, vaping, or vandalism? Art displayed downtown would be gone, no sporting events to attend, no student music would be heard at parades and events. How will our community be known and represented? "These groups teach us valuable life skills that will benefit us long after high school. We gain the opportunity to meet new people that we may not have otherwise. I personally have made amazing friends with some of the best people and have gained much more confidence. These are more than just activities for me, they are family traditions that I hope to carry on. I’m proud to carry the title Bobcat Band member and future Madrigal next year, as my grandmother, Beth Christoff, and other family members were before me. I only hope my brother, sister and future Bowling Green Bobcats have the same opportunities. "We are so lucky to have these activities and experiences, memories, and more. There is something for everyone in our school and it is absolutely vital to the well-being of all students at Bowling Green City Schools and our community. So many participants of these groups have found a safe space or a second home within them. As a result, it makes our school and community a better and much more positive place to be, especially when you know that you always have people behind you to support you. Please continue to support our schools and in turn, our community by voting \'yes\' for the Bowling Green City Schools levy. "Sincerely, Lia Wagner BGHS, Grade 9"', 'Below are some Q&A’s from the campaign trail… I might not be perfectly polished 💅 but I’m smart, I care & I fight when it’s needed 💪 I hope to have earned your vote for the NV State Board of Education ❤️ 🤍 💙 ————————— ❓ What experience or perspective will you bring to the State Board of Education? 👉 I have a plethora of insight into Nevada’s education system. As a student, I attended both CCSD and religious private school. I navigated Head Start and 13 years of public school with both of my children. 15 of my nieces and nephews are currently enrolled in CCSD, and I served on the Clark County Board of Trustees from 2019-2022. The thing that sets me apart and is a valuable asset when working in groups is my ADHD. My divergent thinking allows me to see the big picture, notice things others often miss, come up with out-of-the-box ideas and solutions, and lead passionately and courageously at times like this when authentic, heart-centered leadership is crucial. - - - - - ❓What are your top three priorities? 1. Expand early education and Pre-K programs across the state. Early education is the foundation of a strong educational system. It’s the #1 investment that will have long-term impacts, ensuring our children are better prepared for both school and life. If we want to see real change, we must start at the very beginning. 2. Reduce the number of mandates imposed on educators by the state. Nevada’s teachers are drowning in paperwork and directives, often at the expense of time spent teaching. The current system treats educators as cogs in a machine rather than the experts they are. By reducing state mandates, we can empower educators and create a more sustainable teaching environment, which will help with retention. 3. Reform standardized testing to focus on the whole child. We need a system that reflects the complexity of student growth and development, not one that penalizes educators or students based on a narrow set of metrics. Shifting toward assessments that are more holistic and community-centered will help ensure our education system serves the true needs of Nevada’s students. - - - - - ❓ What do you believe are the most persistent challenges facing the State Board of Education? What is an example of a bold approach you would propose to address one of those challenges? 👉 The biggest challenges facing the Nevada State Board of Education are related to influences outside of Nevada who have a corporate agenda. The solution to this is expanding the board’s network and connecting with other state board members and influences who are not obsessed with standardized testing and corporate reform, and who can advise us in operating in a way that truly supports public schools and the communities they serve. Only with big action towards protecting Public Education, can we fix the individual issues through the state. - - - - - ❓ How do you define student success? 👉 I believe that schools are LEARNING institutions. To me a successful student is one who is showing continued progress in their learning, whether they are below, above, or on pace with their peers. Over the past several decades schools have morphed into TESTING institutions that only place value on math and reading through the use of standardized assessments, which cost school districts millions of dollars each year to use. Our students are not standard and the only people benefiting from the use of standardized tests are the adults who profit from them. If elected, I will advocate for a paradigm shift in which the focus is on teaching kids HOW to learn instead of WHAT to learn. I will push to recognize all subjects, arts, electives, and extra-curriculars with the same importance as is placed on math and reading, and work to ensure every student has access to education and opportunities that allow them to succeed in their areas of interest and passion. - - - - - ❓How will you tackle concerns surrounding school safety for both students and teachers? 👉 School safety needs a comprehensive approach, combining physical safety measures with mental health support. We should ensure schools have appropriate security protocols but also focus on creating environments where students feel supported and connected to their peers and teachers. Increasing access to mental health resources, training staff in conflict resolution and de-escalation, and fostering a culture of open communication can help prevent violence before it starts. Additionally, we must address the root causes of violence, such as bullying and social isolation. - - - - - Remember to vote on or before Election Day: November 5th 🗳️']

Tune in Wednesday and Friday at 4:00 pm MT (6:00 ET) and get the answers you need to live in truth and freedom!!', 'When Israelis went to bed on October 6th, they couldn’t possible imagine they would wake up to a reality that would not only shake the very foundations of our nation, but that it would also transform each and every one of us, marking a before and after in our history as a nation, leaving behind a wound that is still bleeding — exactly one year later today. That terrible day ignited a fire in this nation that no one believed it possessed anymore. A nation of lions rose from the ashes of October 7th, reminded once again that our strength is in our unity, and our foundation is in the unbreakable promises of God that once He brings us back to our ancestral homeland, never again will we be uprooted. As a community of believers in the Land, we saw a tremendous need and knew we had the responsibility and privilege to step up and be a shield and a helping hand in need.
