Name: 51_wildfire_wildfires_fires_firefighting
Representative_Docs: ['For those reading conflicting statements about the fires raging in the Los Angeles area - the current environmental crisis that has risen to the top of the news - the cause of the fires and the response to them can be confusing to say the least. What is clear is that all Southern California is currently in moderate to severe drought (meaning plenty of dry brush and vegetation to act as fuel) and that powerful Santa Ana winds that blow from inland to the coast are fanning the flames. It\'s also clear that people are the cause of most fires in the area - and people have certainly moved into the more natural, hilly parts of Los Angeles County in recent years where these fires are burning. Condolences to those who have lost loved ones and homes. That said, there is another narrative out there that\'s putting the blame in a different direction. Those claims - e.g., that the Los Angeles area fires are being caused by water distribution policies in northern California designed to protect smelt and there’s not enough water to fight fires or there are no FEMA funds available - are being examined by numerous fact-checkers and found to be questionable at best. As one wrote, "Regardless, this wildfire was caused by a combination of a drought, extremely high winds and long-ago decisions by planners and developers about how far into the hills to build..." These environmental crises – from Southern California to South Florida - are going to be with us for a long time to come. We\'ve always had them, but we live in a different, hotter world now (sometimes wetter, sometimes drier, almost always warmer than it was in the past). It\'s going to take knowledge and information to tackle these problems as best as we can. Summary of the political firestorm taking place around the LA fires below including where the political narrative is contradicted by facts. Also - these posts (such as our recent post on offshore drilling) are not meant to promote or disparage politicians or parties. They are intended to encourage discussion and debate on environmental science and policy - what this environmental organization does. There are real questions that should be answered about things like a longer and more intense fire season and more powerful Atlantic hurricanes. Please try to keep comments to the policy aspects of this story (actual causes and recommended responses) as opposed to partisan politics. There is no election coming up so no point whatsoever in campaign-like statements. The question as to who would govern was decided back in early November. But there are certainly environmental problems out there that are going to be with us for a long time that will need to be resolved. https://www.cnn.com/2025/01/09/politics/fact-check-trump-california-wildfires-fema/index.html', 'As I reflect on the recent impact of Hurricane Helene in the Appalachian Mountains, I find myself feeling a mix of gratitude and concern. While our region faced its challenges, I realize that the devastation here, though significant, was nothing in comparison to what our friends on the West Coast are currently experiencing with the wildfires in California. It’s heartbreaking to witness the destruction of landscapes that have stood for centuries, homes already ravaged, and communities facing unprecedented challenges. In the wake of Hurricane Helene, we experienced gusty winds and heavy rain that turned our serene valleys into raging rivers, uprooted trees that have given shade for generations, and caused some damage that will take time and resources to restore. Yet, as the skies cleared and we surveyed the beautiful Appalachian scenery, a sense of resilience washed over me. Nature, in its myriad forms, has a remarkable way of bouncing back, and we too find the strength to rebuild. What has struck me more profoundly, however, is how small our struggles have felt in light of the wildfires raging across California. The images of flames devouring everything in their path, of communities being forced to evacuate, and of the smoke thickening the air have left me in shock. We have seen countless stories of heroes emerging from this disaster—firefighters risking everything on the front lines, neighbors helping one another in times of need, and countless volunteers stepping forward to provide support. Although the wildfires are a reminder of nature’s power and unpredictability, I am inspired by the human spirit that rises above such challenges. It is in moments like these that communities often draw closer together, sharing resources and offering kindness in ways that reaffirms our inherent connections. I think about the families who are finding shelter in makeshift environments, yet still caring for one another, ensuring that no one feels alone during this harrowing time. The contrast between the aftermath of Hurricane Helene and the present tragedy in California reminds us of our responsibilities to each other and to the planet. Events like these serve a purpose—perhaps they remind us of the fragility of our environments and the need for comprehensive strategies to combat climate change that contribute to these increasingly severe weather events and conditions. From where I stand, we are called not just to empathize but to act. While we watch the wildfire devastation unfold, it is crucial to support charities and organizations that are providing assistance to those in need. We can donate, volunteer, and advocate for those who have lost so much. Through unity and humanity, we can spread a glimmer of hope amidst the despair. Moreover, witnessing the resilience displayed by the people affected by the wildfires truly gives me hope. After every storm, both literal and metaphorical, recovery and rebirth are possible. As the fire eventually dwindles down, new beginnings will emerge from the ashes. Nature has a unique ability to heal over time, making way for new growth and opportunities. I have faith that those affected will not only recover but also transform their communities into more accessible, equitable, and sustainable places. While our hearts ache for those fighting the fires and facing evacuations, it’s vital that we remain optimistic. In our own lives, we can plant seeds of hope. Each small act of kindness we offer can help drive the momentum of recovery, knowing that challenges can become catalysts for change. Just as the Appalachian mountains will rise again, so too will the communities devastated by these wildfires, marked by resilience, strength, and renewed purpose. Let us stand together as we navigate the aftermath of disasters in our respective communities. Whether it’s the winds of Hurricane Helene or the firestorm in California, our collective response can be a testament to the strength of the human spirit. May we continue to uplift each other, advocate for sustainable practices, and find ways to be proactive in the face of adversity. Together, we can face whatever storms or wildfires come our way and emerge stronger on the other side.', 'Three Insufferable Talking Heads Bring On $8 Per Gallon Gasoline! The phrase talking head has become slang for a person whose talk is empty and pretentious. Accordingly, allow me to introduce you to three talking heads who are doing their part to ensure gas prices on the Central Coast and throughout CA hit $8 per gallon in less than 2 years by shutting down oil and gas production. The empty pretentious talk associated with this policy initiative? According to Supervisor Capps, by eliminating onshore oil production on the Central Coast, we will never have fires again! That is, she is passing blaming the past fires here on local oil production versus global ghg emissions we can do nothing about. County Supervisor Laura Capps is simply living in a bubble of pretense. She is as woke as they come coupled with an attitude of sanctimonious superiority. She pretends she is going to end wildfires by eliminating oil and gas operations in the county even though most all the devastating wildfires in our region have been started by fallen electrical lines during windstorms which ignited unmanaged chaparral that hadn’t burned in 100 years! The very same electrical lines that are bringing so-called green electricity to our region. Moreover, Capps can’t wrap her head around the difference between production and consumption. As our local and state governments have stymied our state oil production, consumers didn’t reduce consumption; we just imported the oil from half-way around the world to satisfy demand. Hence, the only real thing they accomplished was loss of jobs and resultant higher fuel costs. Supervisor Roy Lee, for his part, is simply a bobble-head. That is, he agrees with virtually everything Capps tells him to do. His one big credibility gap? Uncle Chen’s has gas! What does that mean? It is safe to assume the Chinese restaurant his family owns, called Uncle Chen, is still cooking with the natural gas he wants to eliminate from other people’s homes and businesses. Finally, we have County Supervisor Joan Hartmann, PhD, which must stand for PinheaD. How is it that Hartmann has literally closed her eyes to the fact that shutting down CA oil production will cause more pollution that will arise from shipping oil from halfway around the world instead of pumping it here? Moreover, she believes she has the moral imperative to steal the property rights and investments of people in the oil and gas business even while she is violating the Constitution in the process. Moreover, unlike imported foreign oil, California-produced oil is specially formulated, processed and mitigated to lower its environmental impact. We call it the California blend. Moreover, oil companies and refineries, along with industry, are required to pay money into the California Cap and Trade program, some $33 billion to date, to offset greenhouse gases. That means their ghg footprint has been eliminated by offsetting the emissions. Thereby, these funds are used by the state to eliminate emissions by such things as buying electric buses and, of course, funding the high-speed rail project boondoggle! Hence, CA oil and gas production is not contributing to climate change. Supervisor Capps, aka bubblehead, would have you believe that the $7 million in tax revenues generated in Santa Barbara County is not worth considering as she tries to eliminate the industry. Moreover, she also presented a chart to support her claim that the industry is on its death bed anyway. Her claim is a convenient lie! For starters, she ignored the fact that there was a resurgence of onshore oil production in our county beginning around 2012, when four independent oil operators sought to increase production but were effectively denied the opportunity to do so by the county via arbitrary and capricious regulatory standards! Hence, Capps would have you believe the industry is in decline when the county (and the state) have been responsible for decreased production and, subsequently, reduced refinery capacity. On the California coast alone, the oil industry generates some $10.5 billion in total economic contribution, including $2 billion in tax revenue, and $2 billion in wages and benefits. Furthermore, Californians consume over one billion gallons of gasoline every month! Our gas prices are typically $2 more per gallon than in other states. That means that consumers could have upwards of $26 billion more in their pockets every year to spend on food, rent, and over-priced green utilities, if our cheap-talking politicians would quit their meaningless war against the oil and gas industry. All of this is par for the course for these hard-core leftist talking heads! They steal things without compunction. First, it was the ambulance contract. Now it is the mineral rights of oil royalty owners which is blatantly unconstitutional. For them, the ends justify the means as they seek to force consumers into electric vehicles who won’t be able to afford $8 per gallon gasoline way ahead of our ability to facilitate the transition. Andy Caldwell COLAB']

Thanks to $7.9M from Joe Biden’s Clean School Bus Program, Philly schools double their electric bus fleet to 40! 🚍💨🌱#CleanEnergy Learn more: https://boomtownusa.us/philadelphia-schools-receive-7-9m-for-20-electric-buses-doubling-fleet/', 'Should more NH towns replace diesel school buses with electric school buses? Concord is set to receive three electric school buses by 2026. These buses are part of a federal initiative to replace diesel buses, which contribute to pollution. The event celebrating the upcoming buses highlighted health benefits, cost savings, and environmental advantages. New Hampshire will receive significant federal funding to support this transition to electric buses across the state. Read more: https://www.concordmonitor.com/electric-school-bus-new-hampshire-56521595', 'Register: https://bit.ly/3UBGnk9 Join us to learn firsthand accounts of the benefits of transitioning your fleet to electric, about available funding options, and what it takes to electrify a depot from Pennsylvania districts that have electrified their buses. Electric school buses not only result in reduced greenhouse gas emissions and cleaner air; they also save on maintenance and fuel costs, can provide power back to the grid or buildings during emergencies, and have been linked to improved academic outcomes. Speakers include: - Joshua Dziubek, PA Department of Environmental Protection - Sydney Stern and Charlotte Leon, EPA Region 3 - James Whitesel, Unionville Chadds Ford School District - Mark “J.J.” Carnes, Steelton-Highspire School District - Claire Alford, Highland Electric Fleets - Kevin Matthews, First Student This event is brought to you by Community Advocates for Clean Energy, Generation 180 and Sierra Club Pennsylvania Chapter’s Clean Communities Project.']

Name: 53_food waste_composting_compost_waste
Representative_Docs: ['Happy International Day of Zero Waste! In observance of today, let us tell you about our Zero Food Waste Initiative 🌍♻️ Park City Community Foundation launched Zero Food Waste because food waste is one of our biggest, most solvable climate challenges. It’s simple, achievable, and something we can take action on right now—without waiting on government policies or massive infrastructure changes. 🚨 Why does food waste matter? ❌ Food waste in landfills = significant methane emissions, a greenhouse gas far worse than CO₂. 💰 Nearly 50% of Summit County’s landfill space is taken up by food waste. A new lined cell will cost taxpayers $3.5M next year—but if we divert all food waste, we could save $1.47M annually and extend the landfill’s lifespan by decades. ✨ How do we fix this? Park City Community Foundation is leading the charge, bringing the right people together, launching a new waste diversion program, and tracking progress to create a replicable model for other communities. Summit County and Park City have already signed the Zero Food Waste Compact, and we’re working to expand efforts county-wide. 🌱 Join the movement! - https://parkcitycf.org/zerofoodwaste/ We’re teaming up with residents, businesses, and local leaders to create a community-driven effort to keep food waste from our landfills and valuable nutrients in the cycle. Small everyday changes can lead to big impact—and together, we’re showing that climate solutions don’t have to be complicated. They just need to start with us. 💚', 'Make composting your New Year’s resolution! With Commonwealth Sustainability’s curbside pickup service, we do the work, you get the credit, and together, we create a cleaner, greener future. Start the year sustainably! Here’s a few reasons you should start composting today! 1) Transform your waste into healthy soil for local farms and your garden: Composting turns food scraps and yard waste into nutrient-rich compost, supporting sustainable farming and healthier homegrown produce while reducing reliance on chemical fertilizers. 2) Cut landfill methane emissions by 50%: Organic waste in landfills generates methane, a potent greenhouse gas. Composting keeps this waste out of landfills, helping to combat climate change and improve air quality. 3) Reduce your trash by 30%: By composting, you divert a significant portion of your household waste from the trash bin, lowering disposal costs, conserving landfill space, and making waste management more efficient. We’ll even clean your bin after each pickup, so no more smelly garbage barrels for you! #gardening #westernmass #ecofriendly', 'Introduction: ESG – The New Benchmark for Corporate Value In today\'s world, business success is no longer measured by financial performance alone. Environmental, Social, and Governance (ESG) principles have become critical frameworks for assessing a company\'s long-term resilience, social responsibility, and investment worthiness. For organizations committed to creating a better future, integrating ESG into core operations is not just a responsibility but an opportunity. Our range of advanced Food Waste Disposers embodies this philosophy, serving as a powerful driver and practical manifestation of ESG commitments. Environmental (E): Reducing at the Source, Protecting Our Planet Significantly Diverts Food Waste from Landfills: When conventional food waste decomposes anaerobically in landfills, it generates substantial amounts of methane (CH4) – a potent greenhouse gas with a much higher global warming potential than carbon dioxide. Our disposers grind food scraps (like fruit pits, vegetable trimmings, and leftovers) into fine particles that are safely transported via wastewater to treatment facilities. This fundamentally prevents this organic waste from entering landfills, dramatically reducing methane emissions and directly contributing to climate change mitigation. Alleviates Pressure on Waste Incineration: Food waste is typically high in moisture and low in calorific value. When mixed with other waste for incineration, it reduces efficiency, increases energy consumption, and potentially generates more harmful emissions. By processing food waste at the source, our disposers improve the overall calorific value of waste sent to incinerators, optimizing the combustion process and lowering secondary pollution risks. Enables Resource Recovery: At wastewater treatment plants, the organic particles from disposed food waste can be converted into biogas (a renewable energy source) or nutrient-rich biosolids (used for soil amendment). Our products, in effect, initiate the urban organic recycling chain , actively supporting the development of a circular economy. Reduces Transport Energy & Emissions: Food waste is often heavy and bulky. Reducing its collection frequency and volume directly cuts fuel consumption and exhaust emissions from waste collection vehicles , contributing to improved urban air quality. Social (S): Enhancing Quality of Life, Empowering Healthier Communities Improves Home and Community Hygiene: Storing food waste in indoor bins encourages bacterial growth, attracts pests, and creates unpleasant odors, posing health risks. Disposers enable "instant processing," significantly improving hygiene in home kitchens and communal waste areas, fostering cleaner, healthier living environments , and reducing disease transmission potential. Boosts Convenience and Well-being: Eliminate the hassles of leaks, odors, and frequent trips to take out smelly wet waste. Disposers streamline household chores, enhance daily convenience and comfort , freeing up time and energy. Better residential hygiene directly elevates overall community livability and resident satisfaction. Lessens the Burden on Sanitation Workers: Reducing the volume of wet waste collected, especially minimizing issues like leakage and odor, effectively improves the working conditions for sanitation staff and reduces their physical strain , demonstrating care for frontline community workers. Raises Public Environmental Awareness: Using a disposer is an eco-conscious act in itself. Promoting this product and its environmental benefits helps heighten users\' environmental awareness and sense of responsibility, encouraging the public to adopt source-reduction habits. Governance (G): Innovation Driven, Transparent & Accountable Product R&D & Innovation: We continuously invest in research to enhance disposer performance (grinding efficiency, particle size), energy efficiency (lower operating power), durability (high-quality materials and craftsmanship), and safety (overload protection, splash guards) . Technological advancements embody our commitment to responsible manufacturing and product quality. Sustainable Supply Chain Management: When selecting raw material and component suppliers, we prioritize their environmental compliance, labor standards, and ethical practices , striving to build a responsible supply chain. User Service & Management: We provide clear usage guides, safety instructions, and maintenance advice to ensure correct and safe operation. A robust after-sales service system protects user rights. Furthermore, we actively engage with municipal water authorities and environmental agencies, sharing data and research on the product\'s impact on pipelines and treatment systems, demonstrating transparency and a collaborative spirit. Data Disclosure & Communication: We are committed to disclosing, in future ESG or sustainability reports, quantifiable data on food waste diversion achieved through disposer adoption and the associated estimated carbon reductions , embracing oversight from stakeholders. Conclusion: Choosing Our Food Waste Disposer is Choosing Responsibility and the Future Our advanced Food Waste Disposers are far more than just convenient kitchen appliances. They are vital links connecting households to urban sustainability, effective tools for waste reduction and resource recovery at the source, and tangible solutions for enhancing community environments and resident health. Environmentally, they are pioneers in reducing emissions and protecting ecosystems. Socially, they are ambassadors for improved hygiene, convenience, and care for workers. Governance-wise, they carry our promise of innovation, responsible production, and transparent communication. Investing in our Food Waste Disposers isn\'t just an upgrade to your kitchen; it\'s a smart choice for individuals and organizations to actively practice ESG principles and contribute to building a green, low-carbon, circular society. Let\'s work together, starting from your kitchen, to power a sustainable future and create greater Environmental, Social, and Governance value.']

Trumps REVENGE Plans on Biden and the Dems… His #1 Plan for his first 100 days in office… And the details on a handful of stocks that could skyrocket before trump walks off the Inauguration stage on January 20th. >>> Click Below Now To Watch Jim’s Free Video Now <<<', "THE TRUMP AUTHORITARIAN PLAN TO DESTROY DEMOCRACY Simply stated, the 900 page Project 2025 document is a manifesto for the 'destruction of the administrative state' (Bannon). JSNE urges every American to familiarize with Project 2025, and Take Active Steps to Defeat the Radical Project 2025. Media Matters, a non-profit watchdog, has prepared and updated this readers digest version, to simplify core components for community education. Please share the links in this post with family, friends and associates. ================ MEDIA MATTERS A Guide to Project 2025, the extreme right-wing agenda for the next Republican administration Project 2025 aims to roll back civil rights and destroy the federal government, among other proposals WRITTEN BY SOPHIE LAWTON, JACINA HOLLINS-BORGES, JACK WHEATLEY, JOHN KNEFEL & ETHAN COLLIER PUBLISHED 03/20/24 11:40 AM EDT Project 2025, a comprehensive transition plan organized by right-wing think tank The Heritage Foundation to guide the next GOP presidential administration, is the conservative movement’s most robust policy and staffing proposal for a potential second Trump White House — and its extreme agenda represents a threat to democracy, civil rights, the climate, and more. Project 2025 focuses on packing the next GOP administration with extreme loyalists to former President Donald Trump. The plan aims to reinstate Schedule F, a Trump-era executive order that makes federal employees fireable at-will, stripping tens of thousands of employees of civil service protections. Both Trump and others in the conservative movement have said they will clear out the federal government if he is reelected. The project has even set up online trainings and loyalty tests to narrow down potential hires to those who will commit to follow Trump without question. As Project 2025 senior adviser John McEntee has said, “The number one thing you're looking for is people that are aligned with the agenda.” The Heritage Foundation’s nearly 900-page policy book, titled Mandate for Leadership: A Conservative Promise, describes Project 2025’s priorities and how they would be implemented, broken down by departments in the federal bureaucracy and organized around “four pillars that will, collectively, pave the way for an effective conservative administration: a policy agenda, personnel, training, and a 180-day playbook.” Written primarily by former Trump officials and conservative commentators connected to The Heritage Foundation, these proposals would severely inhibit the federal government’s protections around reproductive rights, LGBTQ and civil rights, climate change efforts, and immigration. The initiative is backed by a coalition of over 100 organizations and individuals, at least two-thirds of which receive funding from the Koch network or conservative philanthropist Leonard Leo. The project is also heavily promoted by MAGA-connected media figures such as Steve Bannon, who has called it the “blueprint” for Trump's second term on his War Room podcast. https://www.mediamatters.org/heritage-foundation/guide-project-2025-extreme-right-wing-agenda-next-republican-administration The Trump campaign has attempted to distance itself from efforts to promote or speculate about “future presidential staffing or policy announcements.” However, Project 2025 is significantly more developed than the Trump campaign’s analog initiative, called Agenda47. And given that the Heritage plan has the backing of virtually the entire conservative movement and links to numerous former Trump officials and advisers, it appears all but inevitable that Trump and his allies will rely on the policies and personnel assembled by Project 2025 if he is reelected in November. This resource outlines the specific policy and personnel priorities of Project 2025 for the next Republican administration. For more of Media Matters' research on Project 2025, click here.", "KEY SIMILARITIES BETWEEN AGENDA 47 AND PROJECT 2025 Both Agenda 47 [1] and Project 2025 [2] are policy platforms associated with Donald Trump's 2024 presidential campaign. After the American People learned about the toxic policies and details of Project 2025, Trump and his campaign team quickly discarded it (which was started in 2021) and announced Agenda 47 in June 2024 [3]. It is no secret that Trump and his campaign “had historically preferred fewer and more vague policy proposals to limit opportunities for criticism and maintain flexibility.” However, Agenda 47 and Project 2025 share many themes and policies; some consider Project 2025 the most detailed look at what a Trump administration would look like [3]. Here are some key similarities between Agenda 47 and Project 2025: 1. EXPANSION OF EXECUTIVE POWER: Both plans emphasize significantly expanding presidential power. ECONOMIC POLICIES: They advocate for protectionist trade policies and aim to restore the production of essential goods in the United States. We can all agree that the second part, “restore the production of essential goods in the United States,” is necessary. But it must be done in a smart, sustainable, and phased manner, not through trade wars with China and other countries that will dramatically increase the price of goods and services. These similarities reflect a shared radical vision that goes beyond a more centralized and conservative approach to governance and institutes an authoritarian government. Representative_Docs: ['Like living on planet Earth 🌍? You’ll love #RankedChoiceVoting! 5 ways RCV could help climate action (from the article): 1 😎 - Electoral upgrades that make our democracy more representative, such as ranked choice voting, can help advance issues like climate policy that are popular with voters. 2 🍃 - Ranked choice voting opens more avenues for issue-based third parties such as the Green Party to showcase their support and proffer candidates without fear of being a spoiler. 3 🙋🏽\u200d♀️- More women and people of color, who tend to care more about climate commitments than average, are more likely to be elected under ranked choice voting, which increases opportunities for action that centers impacted communities that are often underrepresented in climate policy conversations. 4 🤝 - Ranked choice voting decreases political extremism and vitriol, which better supports the collaborative long-term governance necessary for sustainable climate progress. 5 ⚖️ - Multi-winner ranked choice voting can achieve proportional representation, which leads to more climate action. Read more! #RankedChoiceVoting #StrongerByChoice #ClimateAction @[100064604322706:2048:League of Conservation Voters] @[100064606362846:2048:Sunrise Movement] @[100064820167707:2048:350.org] @[100064656675257:2048:Sierra Club] @[100065677771406:2048:Second Act] @[100064289035424:2048:Climate Action Network-International] @[788320473327017:49210:@topfans] @[100064652595505:2048:Sightline Institute]', "Join us on the evening of October 26th, ADC will celebrate Alex Odeh’s life and contributions at the Saturday evening banquet dedicated to his memory. This special program will recognize community members who embody the values Alex championed, including courage, compassion, and a commitment to civil rights. We will spotlight the vital work being carried out by individuals in our community. This event not only pays tribute to his memory but also inspires future generations to carry on the fight for social justice.,Dr. Jill Stein is a Harvard-educated physician and environmental health advocate, known for her work in promoting people-centered policies for the planet and peace. She has led efforts to combat environmental racism and pollution, advocate for healthy communities, and advance democratic reforms. Stein played a key role in securing victories in campaign finance reform, racially just redistricting, and environmental clean-ups. Her push for the Global Climate Convergence emphasized the need for policies that prioritize people and the planet over profit. Stein's disillusionment with mainstream politics, particularly after the repeal of the Clean Election Law in Massachusetts, solidified her alignment with the Green Party. Born in Chicago and raised in Highland Park, Illinois, she graduated magna cum laude from Harvard College and Harvard Medical School.,UCLA Law Review, Northwestern Law Review, the California Law Review and the Harvard Civil Rights and Civil Liberties Law Review, and the Harvard Law Review,Jeanine Erikat is a Program Manager at Women’s Foundation California (WFC), where she oversees the Dr. Beatriz Maria Solís Policy Institute (SPI),an experiential policy advocacy training program advancing racial, economic, and gender justice throughout the state of California. Jeanine is an advisory board member with the Majdal: Arab Community Center in El Cajon and community organizer with the Palestinian Youth Movement. Jeanine holds a B.S. in Public Health Sciences and B.A. in History from UC Irvine, and is currently pursuing a Masters of Public Policy (MPP) at the University of Southern California. Jeanine hopes to work on policy issues that will foster an equitable California for all.,Steve Sosebee is a passionate humanitarian and founder of two impactful nonprofits: HEAL Palestine and The Palestine Children’s Relief Fund (PCRF). For over 30 years, he has provided life-saving medical care to tens of thousands of children in the Middle East, especially in Palestine. Under his leadership, PCRF built pediatric cancer departments, emergency facilities, and developed programs for amputees and mental health care. He has led the treatment of over 2,000 children abroad and deployed thousands of volunteer medical professionals. Steve’s dedication to human rights is deeply rooted in his background in international relations and journalism.", "According to the Green Party, these are the 10 Key Values which would drive the policies of Tre Rook. 1. Grassroots Democracy All human beings must be allowed a say in decisions that affect their lives; no one should be subject to the will of another. We work to improve public participation in every aspect of government and seek to ensure that our public representatives are fully accountable to the people who elect them. We also work to create new types of political organizations that expand the process of participatory democracy by directly including citizens in decision-making. 2. Social Justice And Equal Opportunity As a matter of right, all persons must have the opportunity to benefit equally from the resources afforded us by society and the environment. We must consciously confront in ourselves, our organizations, and society at large, any discrimination by race, class, gender, sexual orientation, age, nationality, religion, or physical or mental ability that denies fair treatment and equal justice under the law. 3. Ecological Wisdom Human societies must function with the understanding that we are part of nature, not separate from nature. We must maintain an ecological balance and live within the ecological and resource limits of our communities and our planet. We support a sustainable society that utilizes resources in such a way that future generations will benefit and not suffer from the practices of our generation. To this end we must practice agriculture that replenishes the soil, move to an energy-efficient economy, and live in ways that respect the integrity of natural systems. 4. Non-Violence It is essential that we develop effective alternatives to society's current patterns of violence. We will work to demilitarize and eliminate weapons of mass destruction, without being naive about the intentions of other governments. We recognize the need for self-defense and the defense of others who are in danger. We promote non-violent methods to oppose practices and policies with which we disagree, and will guide our actions toward lasting personal, community and global peace. 5. Decentralization Centralization of wealth and power contributes to social and economic injustice, environmental destruction, and militarization. We seek a restructuring of social, political and economic institutions away from a system controlled by and mostly benefiting the powerful few, to a democratic, less bureaucratic system. Decision-making should, as much as possible, remain at the individual and local level, while assuring that civil rights are protected for all. 6. Community-Based Economics We support redesigning our work structures to encourage employee ownership and workplace democracy. We support developing new economic activities and institutions that allow us to use technology in ways that are humane, freeing, ecological, and responsive and accountable to communities. We support establishing a form of basic economic security open to all. We call for moving beyond the narrow 'job ethic' to new definitions of 'work,' 'jobs' and 'income' in a cooperative and democratic economy. We support restructuring our patterns of income distribution to reflect the wealth created by those outside the formal monetary economy – those who take responsibility for parenting, housekeeping, home gardens, community volunteer work, and the like. We support restricting the size and concentrated power of corporations without discouraging superior efficiency or technological innovation. 7. Feminism And Gender Equity We have inherited a social system based on male domination of politics and economics. We call for the replacement of the cultural ethics of domination and control with cooperative ways of interacting that respect differences of opinion and gender. Human values such as gender equity, interpersonal responsibility, and honesty must be developed with moral conscience. We recognize that the processes for determining our decisions and actions are just as important as achieving the outcomes we want. 8. Respect For Diversity We believe it is important to value cultural, ethnic, racial, sexual, religious and spiritual diversity, and to promote the development of respectful relationships across the human spectrum. We believe that the many diverse elements of society should be reflected in our organizations and decision-making bodies, and we support the leadership of people who have been traditionally closed out of leadership roles. We encourage respect for all life forms, and increased attention to the preservation of biodiversity. 9. Personal And Global Responsibility We encourage individuals to act to improve their personal wellbeing and, at the same time, to enhance ecological balance and social harmony. We seek to join with people and organizations around the world to foster peace, economic justice, and the health of the planet. 10. Future Focus And Sustainability Our actions and policies should be motivated by long-term goals. We seek to protect valuable natural resources, safely disposing of or 'unmaking' all waste we create, while developing a sustainable economics that does not depend on continual expansion for survival. We must counterbalance the drive for short-term profits by assuring that economic development, new technologies, and fiscal policies are responsible to future generations who will inherit the results of our actions. We must make the quality of all lives, rather than open-ended economic growth, the focus of future thinking and policy. Green Party 10 Key Values: https://www.gp.org/ten_key_values Learn More about Tre Rook: https://rook.vote Make a Plan to Vote: https://BeBallotReady.vote *Remember, vote TWO for State House!* Election Day: November 5th Early Voting: October 9th"]

Name: 60_coal_miners_coal mining_st charles
Representation: ['coal', 'miners', 'coal mining', 'st charles', 'miner', 'coal miners', 'coal plant', 'coal industry', 'coal plants', 'msha']
Representative_Docs: ['“Coal mining first began in what would become West Virginia in the mid-1800s. The bituminous coal mined there became the fuel of choice for industrial production across America. Demand for coal from the fields of West Virginia surged by the century’s end." [...] "Coal miners suffered some of the highest fatality rates among industrial workers. During the early 1900s, scores of miners died from roof collapses, explosions, and fires every year. Large-scale disasters were frequent. The mine safety laws in West Virginia were the weakest in the country, and what laws did exist had few if any provisions to ensure their enforcement.” [...] “The culminating events of the West Virginia mine wars began in late August of 1921, when thousands of [miners] marched 60 miles from the town of Marmet (just outside Charleston, the capitol of West Virginia) to Mingo County where they sought to free striking miners who had been arrested after the governor declared martial law. To get to Mingo, the marchers had to cross Blair Mountain, a mountain ridge defended by the notorious [sheriff] of Logan County, Don Chafin, and his citizen army. The march ended in a violent clash referred to as the Battle of Blair Mountain.” Read more on the history of the West Virginia Mine Wars on the National Park Service website: https://www.nps.gov/articles/000/introduction-to-the-west-virginia-mine-wars.htm *Image: National Park Service | West Virginia & Regional History Center #FlaMineSafety #Miner #MineSafety #MineSafetyTraining #MiningHistory #Miner #MineSafety #MineSafetyTraining #CoalMiner #MSHA', "As Illinois moves toward a cleaner, more sustainable energy future, communities like St. Charles face a pivotal decision that could impact their environmental and financial landscape for decades. Currently, St. Charles, along with other municipalities, is bound by a long-term contract with Illinois Municipal Electric Agency (IMEA) that provides 80% of our power from 2 coal plants. The Prairie State coal plant in Illinois is one of the nation's largest coal polluters and is the #1 CO2 polluters in Illinois. This facility not only contributes millions of tons of CO2 annually but also produces dangerous coal ash that threatens water supplies and public health. This educational event will explore the pressing decision facing St. Charles: extending its contract with IMEA for 20 more years, potentially locking the city into coal-powered electricity until 2055. Attendees will learn about the environmental and health risks associated with Prairie State, the growing availability of cleaner, more affordable energy alternatives, and the role of technology in shaping a sustainable future. Together, we’ll explore how St. Charles can lead the way in making forward-thinking decisions that not only protect the environment but also strengthen our local economy. By making informed, thoughtful choices today, we can create a future where clean energy powers our homes, supports our health, and ensures a thriving community for generations to come. Key Questions to Address: - Is there a business case for St. Charles to sign a new 20 year contract now, 10 years prior to the effective date? - How can St. Charles avoid locking into a contract that may not be in the community's best interest long-term? - What are the environmental and health impacts of continuing to rely on the Prairie State Coal Plant? - What cleaner, cheaper energy options are available as technology advances? Join us for this important discussion and learn how St. Charles can lead the way toward a cleaner, healthier energy future for all. This is a chance to ensure the city's energy decisions align with the rapidly evolving energy landscape.", 'Coal has powered America for centuries, fueling industries, strengthening our economy, and ensuring our energy independence. Yet, it continues to be unfairly maligned by policymakers and activists who push unreliable energy alternatives. In this episode of American Potential, host David From sits down with Mitesh Thakkar, President of Core Natural Resources—the largest coal producer in the U.S.—to set the record straight. Mitesh breaks down the myths surrounding coal, from outdated misconceptions about pollution to the false promise that renewables alone can sustain America’s growing energy needs. He explains why coal remains an essential part of our infrastructure, supporting industries like steelmaking, cement production, and manufacturing—sectors that drive job creation and economic prosperity. With energy demand skyrocketing due to AI, data centers, electric vehicles, and industrial growth, the reckless shutdown of coal plants threatens our grid reliability, national security, and economic stability. Mitesh also exposes the hypocrisy of shutting down domestic coal production while exporting it abroad, where countries like China and India use it to fuel their own industrial booms. America is blessed with abundant natural resources, and we must harness them to ensure affordable, reliable, and secure energy for future generations. Tune in for a powerful conversation on why coal is not the past—it’s the future of American strength, independence, and prosperity. Subscribe now and join the fight for energy freedom!']

Name: 62_virginia_virginia tech_va_roanoke
Representative_Docs: ['Today is Relaxation Day! @[100086631969700:2048:Richmond Home] can relax with more clean energy. Big win for @richmond! Folks! We love seeing solar panels on parking lots, carports, and roofs! The Richmond Flying Squirrels, a Virginia minor league baseball team, are contracting @[100068828755909:2048:Dominion Energy] to install more than 1,700 solar panels at the club’s future park. Those panels will be atop the stadium’s two-tiered roof, and will pair with solar installed at carports in the adjacent lot.', 'A General Assembly bill proposing to standardize what criteria localities are to use when evaluating applications for new solar farms is making its way through the Senate after narrowly passing the House of Delegates. Currently, town and city councils and county boards of supervisors have broad discretion in the length of time they take and the criteria they use to evaluate solar farm applications. What do you think about the state taking this authority away?', 'Hey, Troy Miller here, Democratic candidate for West Virginia House of Delegates District 98, here in Jefferson County. I want to quickly address what is probably the hottest issue in Jefferson County. I don\'t have enough time in this video to go fully in-depth... And that is these blanket permits, and the proliferation of solar "farms" in Jefferson County without community insight, without community input, and opening up to 80% of Jefferson County\'s land for the zoning for the solar "farms" that will produce energy for data centers in northern Virginia -- not locally. And what\'s even more offensive to me now, is that they\'re coming in, and the least one of these projects is basically saying they can\'t move forward without a 39-year tax break. To put that in context -- that\'s a tax break that, if it had been implemented in 1985, it would be expiring this year (2024). There is no reason for this. We need elected officials who will put people over profits and community values over stockholder valuation. I not only oppose corporate handouts to companies that will eventually create natural monopolies in our county for them to create profits that won\'t stay in our county; I support a 21st century Economic Bill of Rights that affirmatively fights for these rights -- this list of economic rights for individuals. Because we need to put people over profits. My name is Troy Miller and I\'m running for House of Delegates in District 98 here in Jefferson County and find out a lot more about me and my campaign *for people* at www.TroyForWV.com. There you can learn more and sign up for updates, to volunteer, and to DONATE! We can\'t take this fight to Charleston without YOUR support! Thank you so much for tuning in and I look forward talking to you down the line!']

Name: 64_superfund_polluters pay_superfund act_climate superfund
Representative_Docs: ["We did! Governor Hochul just signed the New York Climate Superfund bill into law! This is a huge victory for all New Yorkers in our ongoing fight against climate change. 👏 🗽 This historic legislation will require fossil fuel companies to pay $75 billion over 25 years ($3 billion annually) to fund climate resilience projects across New York. The money will help communities protect against flooding, extreme heat, and storms – and because the law is based on past sales, oil companies can't pass these costs on to consumers. This victory would not have been possible without the tireless work of advocates in Albany and activists on the ground across the state of New York and the whole country. Thank you for taking action with us to make this possible. ❤️ This is just the beginning of holding polluters accountable for the damages they’ve caused to our communities. #MakePollutersPay", "On Friday, New Yorkers gathered at Governor Kathy Hochul's office to demand she sign the Climate Superfund Act. Climate change has already caused extraordinary harm in New York. Last summer alone, wildfire smoke blanketed skies and record flooding filled the city subways. The New York Public Interest Research Group put out a report detailing the need for $1.8 billion taxpayer dollars a year for climate projects in New York City. Climate crises are wreaking havoc. Lawmakers in New York want fossil fuel companies to pay for the cost of damages. New legislation in the state, called “Climate Change Superfund Act,” would charge Big Oil companies a one-time fee based on their historic emissions. Show your support by taking action through the link in our bio. @fff.nyc @climatefamsnyc @ny4change @350brooklyn @350nyc @fwwnewyork @nypirg @risenresistnyc @surfridernyc #MakePollutersPay #ClimateSuperfund #EndFossilFuels #ClimateAction #ExtremeHeat #NewYork", 'Carrboro officials filed a landmark lawsuit Wednesday afternoon against Duke Energy Corp., accusing the utility giant of orchestrating a decades-long climate deception campaign. The suit, filed in Orange County Superior Court, claims Duke Energy misled the public about fossil fuels’ role in the climate crisis, delaying a transition to renewable energy and costing the town millions. At a press conference, Mayor Barbara Foushee called the move a stand against environmental injustice. “Historically underserved and marginalized communities are facing disproportionate impacts and health risks that are associated with climate change,” Foushee said. “This was not an easy decision to make, but I believe that we must be courageous as we call out these injustices and seek change and accountability.” The lawsuit highlights Duke Energy’s alleged strategy to block climate action while profiting from fossil fuels, despite knowing their dangers for over 50 years. Mayor Pro Tem Danny Nowell said, “It’s time for us to hold Duke Energy accountable for decades of deception, padding executives’ pockets while towns like ours worked to mitigate the harmful effects of climate change. This suit will allow the Town of Carrboro to invest new resources into building a stronger, more climate-resilient community.” For more news like this, be sure to follow @cardinalandpine']

Name: 67_seafood_aquaculture_island institute_oysters
Representative_Docs: ["🏴\U000e0075\U000e0073\U000e006d\U000e0065\U000e007f The Gulf of Maine is warming faster than 99% of the ocean. There’s no quick-fix for seafood harvesters in the Gulf, but climate solutions DO exist. 🦞 From encouraging species diversification to actively involving frontline communities, changemakers are leading a new path for seafood in Maine. 🐟 Produced by @[100064435974311:2048:SAGE] and @[61557632078329:2048:Seaworthy Experiences], IN HOT WATER explores seafood and climate justice in distinct regions—Season 2 is all about MAINE and out today! 𝗟𝗜𝗦𝗧𝗘𝗡 to the podcast on our site ➡️ bit.ly/3xiNDbO ⬅️OR on Apple Podcasts, Spotify, Amazon Music, Audacy, and Audible! 𝗘𝗣𝗜𝗦𝗢𝗗𝗘 𝟭 ⛈️ Hear from researchers at the Gulf of Maine Research Institute, the University of Maine, the Island Institute, and people working on the water who share challenges faced by the Gulf of Maine’s fisheries and coastal communities: invasive green crabs, sea level rise, coastal erosion, ocean acidification, increasing 100-year storms, and aging wastewater treatment facilities. 𝗘𝗣𝗜𝗦𝗢𝗗𝗘 𝟮 🐋 Learn about the state's iconic lobster fishery, dive into the plight of the endangered right whale, and understand how the present and future of these critically important species are intertwined. 𝗘𝗣𝗜𝗦𝗢𝗗𝗘 𝟯 🦪 Dive into the history of the state's aquaculture sector, kelp farming as a means to diversify income, social license, and the increasing effects of NIMBY (Not in My Backyard), which is becoming more pervasive across the Vacationland state. 𝗘𝗣𝗜𝗦𝗢𝗗𝗘 𝟰 🎣 Learn about working waterfronts, the importance of their preservation for the future of the state's fisheries, gentrification of Maine's coastal communities, and how climate change is endangering the physical and mental health of harvesters and their families. 𝗘𝗣𝗜𝗦𝗢𝗗𝗘 𝟱 🌿 Is seaweed a climate solution? Let's dig into the growth of the seaweed sector in Maine—including both wild and farmed seaweeds! 𝗘𝗣𝗜𝗦𝗢𝗗𝗘 𝟲 ☀️ Hear from Plansowes Dana, a member of the Passamaquoddy Tribe and the Indigenous Communities Partnership Manager at the Gulf of Maine Research Institute. Her goal is to build relationships between the Wabanaki communities and GMRI to incorporate Indigenous knowledge with Western science.", 'Join us at Maine Beer Company in Freeport on September 24 for FREE beer 🍺 , oysters 🦪 , and a screening 📽️ of our latest short film, Aquaculture for People and Planet! Discover how Maine’s shellfish and seaweed industries are leading the way in climate-friendly food production. Aquaculture for People and Planet, the latest short film in our Climate of Change series, explores how Island Institute is helping Maine’s seafood businesses lower their carbon footprint, boost resilience, and build a thriving, sustainable seafood economy. Stick around after the screening for a panel discussion with local aquaculturists and Island Institute staff, where we’ll dive into the vast potential of low-emission seafood, Maine’s opportunity to lead in this growing industry, and the innovative ways sea farmers are cutting their carbon footprint while increasing their bottom line. We’ll also explore why farmed shellfish and seaweed are among the most efficient, sustainable protein sources on the planet—offering a scalable solution for both the economy and the environment. Reserve your seat today here: https://bit.ly/3FVsKI6 Featured Speakers: 🔹Willy Leathers, Owner, Maine Ocean Farms, Brunswick, ME 🔹Matt Moretti, Owner, Bangs Island Mussels, Portland, ME 🔹Sam Feldman, Community Development Officer, Island Institute, Rockland, ME Moderator: 🔹Sam Belknap, Director, Center for Marine Economy, Island Institute, Rockland, ME Doors open at 6:30pm with the screening starting promptly at 7:00pm.', "Profs and Pints Northern Virginia presents: “Buying Sustainable Seafood,” or how to shop like a marine biologist, with David Shiffman, marine conservation biologist, research associate at Arizona State University's Washington D.C. center, lecturer in environmental studies at Georgetown University, and author of the forthcoming book The Science of Sustainable Seafood: A Guide for Concerned Consumers. [5:30 pm February 23rd at Crooked Run Fermentation in Sterling, Va.] Growing numbers of Americans are feeling concerned by the environmental impacts of their meals and looking to sustainable seafoods as healthy and eco-friendly choice for dinner. With so many environmental impacts associated with so many different kinds of fishing, however, it can be incredibly difficult to figure out what fish options are actually sustainable. Adding to the confusion, many of the leading figures and organizations offering advice disagree on what sustainable means and offering conflicting recommendations about the same species of fish. The Atlantic blacktip shark, for example, has been listed as green/best choice by NOAA FishWatch and red/worst choice by the Environmental Defense Fund's Seafood Selector. Some influential activists argue that no seafood is sustainable, a claim popularized by Netflix’s SeaSpiracy, and say that anyone who cares about the environment should avoid seafood entirely. Making matters worse is outright deception by some seafood harvesters and sellers. Because no government regulations govern what products deserve the label, some industry groups wrongly describe as sustainable seafood that is far from it. Some grocery stores out there sell extremely unsustainable products and even actively mislead people by greenwashing with false sustainability claims. Join marine conservation biologist David Shiffman as he helps clear the waters surrounding sustainable seafood consumption. He'll discuss the different definitions and standards for sustainable seafood, teach you how to read a sustainability label like a fisheries biologist, and challenge some of the most common misconceptions about sustainable seafood, overfishing, and ocean conservation. You'll learn how to avoid being misled by grocery stores and industry groups, and pick up tips and tricks for finding healthy, delicious seafood that is sustainable as well. (Advance tickets: $13.50 plus sales tax and processing fees. Doors: $17, or $15 with a student ID. Listed time is for doors. Talk starts 30 minutes later.)"]

Name: 68_clinton county_wind farms_wind_wind energy
Representative_Docs: ['It’s harvest season in the Land of Lincoln! Be sure to watch for equipment on the roads. Farmers, we hope you have a fruitful harvest – stay safe out in the fields. 🌾 We’re proud to support farmers during fall harvest and beyond. Wind turbines can be easily farmed around, and many farmers report that wind farm access roads are really helpful for traversing their fields and hauling crops out. Participating in a renewable energy project allows farmers to hold agricultural land in place for the next generation while receiving a stable, consistent source of income.', 'We recently had the pleasure of speaking with Kendall and Virginia Rodgers, who shared their insightful perspective on the many benefits wind energy provides. See their first-hand experience of how wind energy can be a sustainable and beneficial addition to local farms and communities. #FarmerFriday "We’ve lived here on our 40-acre property for about 30 years now. It’s been a peaceful life, surrounded by familiar faces and the beauty of the land. When the wind turbines were first proposed, we were supportive right from the start. To us, it just made sense, and we saw the potential benefits for the community. What really stood out to us was how the turbines could help our neighbors financially. A lot of them were able to lease their land for the turbines, and we were just happy to see them making some extra money off it. It’s hard enough to maintain land and make a living out here, so every bit helps. We’ve always believed that if something can benefit the community without causing harm, it’s a good thing. We’ve never had any concerns about the turbines. Honestly, we don’t even hear them. \'They are just there, and we often enjoy the view,\' as Virginia says. You can see the turbines from our home, and it’s kind of nice, actually. They’ve just become part of the landscape, something familiar and even comforting. One of the things we also appreciate is how these turbines help with funding for the local schools. Our area isn’t the wealthiest, so anything that can contribute to better resources for the kids is important. We’re just happy to see how much good this project has done, not just for the environment, but for the people living here too." -Kendall and Virginia Rogers, Michigan Landowners #FarmToPower #SupportLocalFarmers #FarmLife #HeartlandFarming #SustainableFarming #ResilientFuture #LocalAg #AgAdvocacy #NextGenFarmers #AgInnovation #FarmersSupportingFarmers #SustainableAgriculture #AgCommunity #FutureOfFarming #AgSustainability #RuralRevitalization #FightFakeNews #SupportFarmers #FarmersRights #EnergyMix', 'It’s harvest season in the Lone Star state! Be sure to watch for equipment on the roads. Farmers, we hope you have a fruitful harvest – stay safe out in the fields. 🌾 We’re proud to support farmers during fall harvest and beyond. Wind turbines can be easily farmed around, many farmers report the wind farm access roads being really helpful for traversing their fields and hauling crops out. Participating in a renewable energy project allows farmers to hold agricultural land in place for the next generation while receiving a stable, consistent source of income.']
